**(b)** State and explain **Fitts's Law** as applied to pointing device evaluation. A user performs a pointing task on a touchscreen. The target is **200 pixels** away and **40 pixels** wide. For this device, the regression coefficients obtained were **a = 100 ms** and **b = 200 ms/bit**. Calculate:
- (i) the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits,
- (ii) the **predicted Movement Time (MT)** in milliseconds, and
- (iii) the **Throughput (TP)** in bits/second, given the empirically observed mean movement time is **550 ms**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Input Devices — Block Diagram**

A block diagram is reproduced here (the textual equivalent of SECTION 4.1):

```
INPUT DEVICES
├── Text Entry Devices (QWERTY Keyboard, Dvorak Keyboard, Chord Keyboard, On-Screen Keypad)
├── Pointing Devices (Mouse, Trackball, Joystick, Touchpad, Light Pen, Touchscreen)
├── Imaging & Sensing Devices (Webcam, Depth Camera, Microphone, IMU, Eye Tracker, Bioelectric Sensors)
└── Physical Controls (Button, Dial, Slider, Multi-Touch Surface, Haptic Glove)
```

**Working Principle of Two Pointing Devices:**

**1. Optical Mouse:**
- The mouse has a **light source (LED or laser)** and a **CMOS image sensor**.
- As the mouse is moved, the surface below reflects light into the sensor at thousands of frames per second.
- A **digital signal processor (DSP)** compares successive images to compute **displacement vectors (ΔX, ΔY)**.
- These deltas are sent to the host computer via USB/Bluetooth, which integrates them to move the cursor on screen.
- Modern optical mice use **laser sensors** with high DPI (1600–25000+) for gaming and design HMI.

**2. Capacitive Touchscreen (Projected Capacitive — PCT):**
- The screen has a grid of **transparent electrodes (Indium Tin Oxide)** driven by an AC voltage.
- A finger (or stylus with conductive tip) placed on the screen forms a **capacitor** with the nearest electrode.
- The change in capacitance at that node is measured, identifying the **X, Y position**.
- Modern PCT supports **multi-touch** by scanning a matrix of rows and columns (mutual capacitance).

**Working Principle of Two Text-Entry Devices:**

**1. QWERTY Mechanical Keyboard:**
- Each key is a switch with **keycap, stem, spring, and contact leaves**.
- Pressing a key completes a **closed circuit**, sending a **scan code** to the keyboard controller.
- Switch types: **membrane, scissor, buckling spring, mechanical (Cherry MX types)**.
- The controller **debounces** the signal and transmits it via USB HID (Human Interface Device) protocol.

**2. Multi-Tap Phone Keypad (12-key):**
- The keypad has 12 physical keys (0–9, *, #).
- Letters are mapped: 2=ABC, 3=DEF, 4=GHI, 5=JKL, 6=MNO, 7=PQRS, 8=TUV, 9=WXYZ.
- To type "HELLO", the user presses: 4-4 (pause) 3-3-3 (pause) 5-5-5 (pause) 5-5-5 (pause) 6-6-6.
- A **timeout** (typically 1 second) signals word boundary.
- **T9 (Text on 9 Keys)** improvement: Press each key *once* per letter; an algorithm disambiguates using a dictionary.

**[Marks Distribution: 2 for diagram, 2 for pointing devices, 3 for text-entry devices]**

---

**(b) Fitts's Law — Solution**

**Statement:**
Fitts's Law (1954, refined by MacKenzie 1992) models the relationship between the **difficulty of a pointing task** and the **time required to complete it**. It is given by:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

where $MT$ is movement time, $D$ is the distance to target, $W$ is the target width, $a$ and $b$ are device-specific regression coefficients, and the logarithmic term is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measured in bits.

**Given:** $D = 200$ pixels, $W = 40$ pixels, $a = 100$ ms, $b = 200$ ms/bit, observed mean $MT = 550$ ms.

**(i) Index of Difficulty (ID):**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{200}{40} + 1\right) = \log_2(5 + 1) = \log_2(6)
$$

Converting to base-2 logarithm:

$$
\log_2(6) = \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.791759}{0.693147} = 2.584963
$$

$$
\boxed{ID \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}}
$$

**(ii) Predicted Movement Time (MT):**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 100 + 200 \times 2.585
$$

$$
MT = 100 + 517.0 = 617.0 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{MT \approx 617 \text{ ms}}
$$

**(iii) Throughput (TP):**

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT_{observed}} = \frac{2.585 \text{ bits}}{0.550 \text{ s}} = 4.700 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{TP \approx 4.70 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A throughput of ~4.7 bits/s is reasonable for a touchscreen (which is generally slower than a mouse, ~5–6 bits/s). Designers can use this number to compare alternative devices for the same task.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 for statement, 1 for setup, 1 for ID, 2 for MT, 2 for TP]**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of computer output devices** in HCI. Describe in detail the working of **LCD and OLED display technologies**, including a comparison of their suitability for mobile and desktop HMI. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **WPM (Words Per Minute)** as a keyboard performance metric. A user types 750 characters in 3 minutes with 18 uncorrected errors. Calculate the typing speed in WPM. Also, discuss the **merits and demerits of the Dvorak keyboard layout** compared to QWERTY. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Output Devices — Explanation**

Computer output devices are classified into three primary sensory channels:

```
OUTPUT DEVICES
├── VISUAL DISPLAYS
│     ├── CRT, LCD, LED, OLED, E-Ink
│     ├── HMD (VR/AR)
│     └── Retinal Projector
├── AUDITORY DISPLAYS
│     ├── Loudspeaker, Headphones, Bone Conduction
│     ├── Sonification Engine
│     └── TTS (Text-to-Speech) Synthesizer
└── HAPTIC / TACTILE DISPLAYS
      ├── Vibrotactile (ERM, LRA)
      ├── Force Feedback
      ├── Ultrasonic Mid-Air Haptics
      └── Electrocutaneous Stimulators
```

**Working of LCD (Liquid Crystal Display):**
- An LCD panel consists of **two polarized glass substrates** with **liquid crystal (twisted nematic) molecules** sandwiched between them.
- The **backlight (LED)** emits white light, which passes through the **first polarizer**, then through the liquid crystal layer.
- Each pixel has a **thin-film transistor (TFT)** that applies a voltage, causing the liquid crystals to *untwist* and block or allow light to pass.
- A **color filter array** (RGB sub-pixels) produces the final color.
- **HMI Suitability:** Excellent for desktop monitors, TVs; power-efficient for laptops. Slower response time, lower contrast.

**Working of OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode):**
- Each pixel is a thin organic compound (**small molecule or polymer OLED**) sandwiched between two electrodes (anode and cathode).
- When current flows, **electrons and holes recombine** in the emissive layer, releasing energy as photons (electroluminescence).
- *No backlight is required* — each pixel generates its own light.
- **HMI Suitability:** Superior contrast (true blacks), wider viewing angles, faster response, flexible/foldable form factors. Ideal for smartphones and premium displays. Concerns: **burn-in**, shorter lifetime of blue sub-pixel.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | LCD | OLED |
|---|---|---|
| Backlight | Required (LED) | Not required (self-emissive) |
| Contrast Ratio | 1000:1 typical | 1,000,000:1 (infinite) |
| Response Time | 1–5 ms | < 0.1 ms |
| Power Efficiency | Constant (always on backlight) | Variable (per-pixel; better on dark UIs) |
| Form Factor | Rigid | Flexible/Foldable possible |
| Burn-in Risk | None | Possible (especially blue) |
| Mobile HMI | Mid-range phones | Premium phones, foldables |
| Desktop HMI | Monitors, productivity | Creative work, video editing |

**[Marks Distribution: 1 for classification, 2 for LCD, 2 for OLED, 2 for comparison]**

---

**(b) WPM Calculation and Dvorak vs QWERTY Discussion**

**WPM Definition:**

$$
WPM = \frac{\text{Characters Typed} - \text{Uncorrected Errors}}{5 \times \text{Time in minutes}}
$$

**Given:** Characters typed = 750, Uncorrected errors = 18, Time = 3 minutes.

**Calculation:**

$$
\text{Net characters} = 750 - 18 = 732
$$

$$
WPM = \frac{732}{5 \times 3} = \frac{732}{15} = 48.8
$$

$$
\boxed{WPM = 48.8 \text{ words per minute}}
$$

This corresponds to an **above-average typist** (typical average is 40 WPM).

**Dvorak vs QWERTY — Merits and Demerits:**

**Merits of Dvorak:**
1. **Ergonomic Efficiency:** All 5 vowels (A, O, E, U, I) and the 5 most common consonants (T, H, S, N, R) are on the **home row** (left hand for vowels, right hand for common consonants). This keeps fingers at the rest position for ~70% of typing.
2. **Reduced Finger Travel:** Studies show ~70% reduction in finger travel distance, reducing Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI).
3. **Balanced Hand Load:** Alternates hands more evenly, reducing one-hand fatigue seen in QWERTY.
4. **Higher Theoretical Speed:** Some studies report 10–20% speed gains for trained Dvorak users.

**Demerits of Dvorak:**
1. **Social Inertia:** QWERTY is the **de facto standard** worldwide; switching has a steep re-learning curve.
2. **Touch-Typing Retraining:** Existing QWERTY typists experience temporary productivity loss.
3. **Hardware Incompatibility:** Most keycaps are labeled for QWERTY; physical key positions still default to QWERTY in OS.
4. **No Universal Standard:** Dvorak Simplified Keyboard (DSK) is one of several variants; no global consensus.
5. **Limited Empirical Support in Practice:** Most controlled studies show only marginal gains; benefits are contested.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 for WPM formula, 2 for calculation, 2 for merits, 2 for demerits]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in "The Computer: Devices" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Fitts's Law — Wrong Form:** Students often write $D/W$ instead of $D/W + 1$ (Shannon form). This causes negative IDs for small distances — guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **ID Unit Confusion:** The ID is in **bits**, not dimensionless. Forgetting the unit loses the conceptual mark.
>
> 3. **WPM Divisor:** Using 6 (gross WPM) instead of 5 (net WPM) is a common error. Examiners check this carefully.
>
> 4. **Affordance vs Feedback Mix-up:** Writing *"button beeps when pressed"* as affordance instead of feedback loses 1 mark. Affordance = what the device *suggests*; Feedback = what it *tells you after* the action.
>
> 5. **Position vs Rate Control Reversal:** Saying "mouse is rate control" is wrong. The mouse's position equals the cursor's position — that's position control.
>
> 6. **Display Technology Confusion:** Writing "LED display" when meaning "LED-backlit LCD" loses accuracy marks. **LED** and **OLED** are different technologies.
>
> 7. **No Block Diagram in Part (a):** Examiners *require* a diagram for classification questions. Missing it costs **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — "The Computer: Devices"**
>
> ✅ **Input Devices** are classified into: *Text Entry, Pointing, Imaging/Sensing, Physical Controls*.
>
> ✅ **Output Devices** are classified into: *Visual, Auditory, Haptic/Tactile*.
>
> ✅ **Fitts's Law (Shannon form):** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — cornerstone of pointing device evaluation.
>
> ✅ **Index of Difficulty:** $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ bits; **Throughput:** $TP = ID / MT$ bits/second.
>
> ✅ **WPM Formula:** $WPM = (\text{Chars} - \text{Errors}) / (5 \times \text{Time in min})$.
>
> ✅ **Position Control:** Device position = Cursor position (mouse). **Rate Control:** Device motion *rate* = Cursor motion rate (joystick in flight sim).
>
> ✅ **Affordance** is what a device *suggests* you can do; **Constraint** *limits* actions; **Mapping** links control to effect; **Feedback** confirms action results.
>
> ✅ **LCD** needs backlight, lower contrast; **OLED** is self-emissive, infinite contrast, supports folding.
>
> ✅ **QWERTY** was designed to *prevent* jamming (historical artifact). **Dvorak** is theoretically superior but socially locked out.
>
> ✅ **T9 / Multi-tap:** Predictive text algorithms for 12-key phone keypads.
>
> ✅ **Standard performance benchmarks:** Mouse ~4.5–6.0 bits/s; Touchscreen ~2.5–4.0 bits/s; Average typist ~40 WPM; Professional ~70+ WPM.
>
> ✅ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** govern device-driven interface consistency, feedback, and reversibility.
>
> ✅ **Haptic feedback budget** must be < 20 ms; **Display refresh** typically 60 Hz (VR needs ≥ 90 Hz).
>
> ✅ **Multi-modal HMI** integrates speech + gesture + touch for natural interaction — the future of device design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — MEMORY
## Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Memory (Cognitive HMI Perspective)**
> **Memory** is the cognitive faculty of the human information-processing system responsible for **encoding**, **storing**, and **retrieving** sensory input, motor programs, semantic knowledge, and episodic experiences over varying temporal scales. In the context of Advanced Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI), memory defines the **capacity, duration, and accessibility boundaries** within which a human operator can perceive, hold, manipulate, and act upon information exchanged with a machine.

From the **information-processing model** of human cognition, memory is not a single monolithic store but a **hierarchical, multi-store architecture** with distinct physiological substrates, decay characteristics, and retrieval mechanisms. The three canonical stores are:

1. **Sensory Memory** (Iconic / Echoic / Haptic) — sub-second buffer.
2. **Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory (WM)** — seconds to minutes, capacity-limited.
3. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** — minutes to lifetime, effectively unlimited capacity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Memory as a Three-Tier Office Filing System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy for HMI Students**
> Imagine the human mind as a **three-room office**:
> - **Room 1 — The Reception Desk (Sensory Memory):** Every visitor (stimulus) walks past the desk. The receptionist glances at each one for **less than a second** before they leave. Only those who "catch the eye" are invited further inside.
> - **Room 2 — The Workbench (Short-Term / Working Memory):** A small workbench that can hold only about **7 items** at a time (Miller's Law). You actively manipulate items here — assembling, comparing, calculating. If you stop working, items drop off the bench within **15–30 seconds** unless rehearsed.
> - **Room 3 — The Archive Library (Long-Term Memory):** A vast, permanent library with unlimited shelves. Information is filed by **meaning, association, and schema**. Retrieval is by search, not by position.

This analogy is **directly exploited in HMI design**: machines must push the right stimuli to the right "room" at the right time. Overloading the workbench leads to **cognitive overload**; under-staffing the archive leads to **learnability failure**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Cognitive Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following values are the **empirically established benchmarks** used in HMI cognitive modeling. Memorize these for Part A questions.

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit / Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Miller's Magic Number (STM capacity)** | **7 ± 2** | chunks |
| **Cowan's Revised Capacity (WM)** | **4 ± 1** | chunks |
| **Iconic Memory Duration** | **0.25 – 0.50** | seconds |
| **Echoic Memory Duration** | **3 – 4** | seconds |
| **STM Duration (without rehearsal)** | **15 – 30** | seconds |
| **LTM Capacity** | effectively **unlimited** | — |
| **Rehearsal Rate (articulatory loop)** | **≈ 2** | seconds per chunk |
| **Reading Speed (avg. adult)** | **250** | wpm |
| **Long-term Potentiation (LTP) onset** | **< 100** | ms (neural) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these matter in HMI:** Every interface guideline — menu depth ≤ 3, error message length ≤ 12 words, single-screen options ≤ 7 — is a direct consequence of these constants. A designer who violates them forces the operator to drop information off the workbench.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 The Multi-Store Model (Atkinson–Shiffrin, 1968)

The classical, KTU-prescribed model partitions human memory into the three sequential stores. Information flows serially under **attention control**, with **rehearsal** acting as the only bridge from STM to LTM.

**Operational Steps of the Multi-Store Model:**

1. **Sensory Registration (Pre-attentive Stage):** Raw physical stimuli (light, sound, pressure) hit the corresponding sensory register and decay in **< 1 second** unless the attentional spotlight selects them.
2. **Attentional Transfer:** Selected stimuli cross into **STM** under control of the central executive. This transfer is **capacity-limited** — only the attended channel passes.
3. **Encoding & Rehearsal:** In STM, information is encoded primarily in **phonological (verbal)** form for auditory material and **visual-spatial sketchpad** for spatial material. Maintenance rehearsal (silent repetition) refreshes the trace every **~ 2 seconds**.
4. **Consolidation to LTM:** Elaborative rehearsal — linking new info to pre-existing LTM schemas — promotes transfer to LTM. This is the **learning step** in HMI skill acquisition.
5. **Retrieval:** LTM contents are activated by **cues** (recognition, recall, or reconstruction) and brought back into WM for task execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" matters for HMI:** Every usability heuristic (Nielsen's visibility of system status, match between system and real world) is a manipulation of one of these five steps. Forgetting passwords (LTM retrieval failure) and missing menu items (WM overload) are textbook symptoms of model violation.

---

### 2.2 Baddeley's Working Memory Model (1974, Revised 2000)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **updated multi-component working memory model** because it is the dominant cognitive architecture used in modern HMI workload assessment (e.g., NASA-TLX mapping, GOMS analysis).

**Components:**

- **Phonological Loop** — handles verbal/acoustic information. Subdivided into:
  - *Phonological Store* (inner ear) — capacity ≈ 2 s of speech.
  - *Articulatory Rehearsal Process* (inner voice) — refreshes the store.
- **Visuospatial Sketchpad** — handles visual and spatial information. Subdivided into:
  - *Visual Cache* (form, colour).
  - *Inner Scribe* (motion, sequence).
- **Episodic Buffer** (added 2000) — integrates information from the other two components and LTM into a **unified episodic representation**. Capacity ≈ 4 chunks.
- **Central Executive** — attentional controller, no storage capacity. Allocates processing resources, switches tasks, inhibits irrelevant stimuli.

---

### 2.3 Long-Term Memory Subdivisions (Tulving, 1972; Squire, 1992)

LTM is **declarative (explicit)** vs **non-declarative (implicit)**:

| Subtype | Sub-subtype | HMI Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Declarative / Explicit** | Episodic (events, time-stamped) | Recall of past interaction sessions |
| | Semantic (facts, concepts) | Menu structure comprehension |
| **Non-Declarative / Implicit** | Procedural (skills, habits) | Motor skill on a touchscreen, typing |
| | Priming | Auto-completion suggestion acceptance |
| | Classical Conditioning | Alarm → response association |

---

### 2.4 Memory Processes: Encoding, Storage, Retrieval

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Memory (KTU Mandated Framework)**

1. **Encoding** — Transformation of perceptual input into a storable neural representation.
   - Levels-of-Processing (Craik & Lockhart): *shallow* (orthographic) → *deep* (semantic). **Deeper processing = stronger memory.**
2. **Storage** — Maintenance of the encoded trace over time.
   - Affected by interference (proactive/retroactive), decay, and consolidation (hippocampal → cortical).
3. **Retrieval** — Reactivation of stored information.
   - *Recall* (no cue, hardest) — typing password from memory.
   - *Recognition* (cue present, easiest) — selecting correct icon.
   - *Reconstruction* (fragments, errors likely) — narrating a tutorial.

**HMI Implication:** Interfaces that rely on **recall** (command-line interfaces) are harder to learn than those relying on **recognition** (GUIs with menus/icons). This is the empirical basis of **Nielsen's heuristic #6: Recognition rather than recall.**

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Quantitative Relations in Memory Theory**

| # | Relation | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Miller's Law (Ideal)** | $C = 7 \pm 2$ | $C$ = STM capacity in chunks |
| 2 | **Cowan's Revised Capacity** | $C_{WM} = 4 \pm 1$ | $C_{WM}$ = working memory chunks |
| 3 | **Decay Function (STM, exponential)** | $R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$ | $R$ = recall probability, $\tau \approx 18\,\text{s}$ |
| 4 | **Rehearsal Refresh Period** | $T_{r} \approx 2\,\text{s}$ | $T_r$ = articulatory loop cycle |
| 5 | **Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve** | $b = \dfrac{1.25 \cdot \log(t)}{1 + 0.15 \cdot t}$ | $b$ = savings (%), $t$ = days |
| 6 | **Interference Strength (Underwood)** | $I = k \cdot (N_{pre} + N_{post})$ | $N_{pre}$, $N_{post}$ = similar items before/after |
| 7 | **Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = decision time, $n$ = choices |
| 8 | **Fitts' Law (Motor Targeting)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width |
| 9 | **Effective LTM Transfer** | $P_{LTM} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot E}$ | $E$ = elaborative rehearsal count, $\lambda$ = constant |
| 10 | **Recognition vs Recall Error** | $E_{rec} \approx 0.05 \cdot E_{recall}$ | ratio approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas feed directly into **GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection)** and **KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)** analyses used to predict operator throughput in safety-critical HMIs (aviation cockpits, ICU monitors, nuclear control rooms). The exponential decay in equation 3 explains why **status displays** must be persistently visible, not flashed once.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aviation Glass Cockpits:** Menu structures limited to 4–5 items per level (WM constraint). Persistent PFD/ND displays (decay mitigation).
- **Medical Infusion Pumps:** Confirmation step relies on **recognition** (drug name + dose displayed) to overcome stress-induced recall failure.
- **Smartphone UI:** Auto-complete and predictive text exploit **priming** and **recognition** to offload WM.
- **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Audio + visual redundant alerts leverage the **dual-coding hypothesis** (Paivio) for stronger LTM encoding.
- **VR/AR Training Simulators:** Spaced repetition schedules follow the **spacing effect** (Ebbinghaus) to maximize LTM consolidation of procedural skills.
---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Derivation — STM Decay Function from First Principles

The standard empirical decay of a short-term memory trace, in the absence of rehearsal, can be modeled as a **first-order linear differential equation** governing the strength $R(t)$ of the neural trace.

**Step 1 — Define the decay hypothesis.**
Memory trace strength decreases in proportion to its current magnitude:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -k \cdot R(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**
The negative sign indicates decay. Integrating from $t = 0$ (initial trace strength $R_0$) to time $t$:

$$
\int_{R_0}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R_0 = -k t
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides to isolate $R(t)$.**

$$
R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the time constant.**
The time constant $\tau$ is defined as the time at which $R(t)$ drops to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of $R_0$, which gives $k = 1/\tau$. Empirically, **Peterson & Peterson (1959)** measured $\tau \approx 18$ s for trigrams.

**Step 6 — Final canonical form (KTU formula).**

$$
R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau}, \quad \tau \approx 18\;\text{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic for students:** This exponential form is identical to the **RC circuit discharge** equation in electrical engineering. The KTU examiner expects you to draw the analogy: trace strength ↔ capacitor voltage, rehearsal ↔ charging current, decay constant ↔ $1/RC$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Rehearsal-Bounded Effective STM Duration

**Step 1 — Define rehearsal as a discrete refresh event.**
Every $T_r \approx 2$ s, the articulatory loop restores $R$ to a fraction $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 0.95$) of its peak.

**Step 2 — Net trace strength after one cycle.**
Starting from $R_0$, after time $T_r$ the trace has decayed to $R_0 e^{-T_r/\tau}$. The rehearsal adds back $\alpha$ of what was lost:

$$
R_1 = \alpha \cdot R_0 \left(1 - e^{-T_r/\tau}\right) + R_0 e^{-T_r/\tau}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state (after $n$ cycles).**
Solving the recurrence $R_{n+1} = R_n e^{-T_r/\tau} + \alpha R_0 (1 - e^{-T_r/\tau})$ gives a geometric series whose limit is:

$$
R_{\infty} = \dfrac{\alpha \, R_0 \, (1 - e^{-T_r/\tau})}{1 - e^{-T_r/\tau}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for standard parameters.**
With $\tau = 18$ s, $T_r = 2$ s, $\alpha = 0.95$:

$$
e^{-2/18} = e^{-0.1111} \approx 0.8948
$$

$$
R_{\infty} = \dfrac{0.95 \cdot R_0 \cdot 0.1052}{0.1052} = 0.95 \, R_0
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Continuous maintenance rehearsal can sustain STM at **~95% of initial strength** indefinitely — the cognitive basis of "holding a phone number in mind by repeating it."

> [!NOTE]
> **HMI translation:** This is why **confirmation dialogs that require the user to re-type a value** (active rehearsal) outperform those that merely display a checkbox (passive review). The motor act of re-entry acts as the refresh impulse.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Fitts' Law Application to HMI Targeting

Although Fitts' Law is strictly a **motor control** law, it is included because it sets the lower bound on menu/icon dimensions and depends on the **perceptual-memory** decision time as a constant offset.

**Step 1 — State Fitts' Law.**

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the minimum target width given an SLA.**
For a 2 s response SLA and empirical $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit, target at $D = 100$ mm from cursor:

$$
2.0 = 0.050 + 0.150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{100}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the logarithm.**

$$
\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{100}{W} + 1\right) = \dfrac{2.0 - 0.050}{0.150} = 13.0
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate (base 2) and solve for $W$.**

$$
\dfrac{100}{W} + 1 = 2^{13.0} = 8192
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{100}{8191} \approx 0.0122 \text{ mm} = 12.2\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Realistic revision.**
Since $12\;\mu\text{m}$ is below human perceptual resolution, this confirms the law's value as a **lower bound** — designers should still respect the **two-pixel minimum** convention for any clickable target, regardless of Fitts' calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU valuation error:** Students often write $D/W$ inside the log without the $+1$ safeguard. Without the $+1$, the argument becomes undefined when $D < W$ (target wider than distance). Always include the $+1$ — this is the **Shannon formulation** of Fitts' Law and is what the KTU marking scheme rewards.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Failure Modes and HMI Mitigations

| Memory Store | Failure Mode | Cognitive Symptom | HMI Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensory | Masking | Brief flash missed | Persistent display, redundant alerts |
| STM (Phonological) | Decay in 18 s | Phone number forgotten | Auto-fill, copy-to-clipboard |
| STM (Visuospatial) | Capacity overflow (≥ 5 items) | User cannot track all dashboard tiles | Progressive disclosure, tabbed views |
| Working Memory | Interference from prior task | Mode error (wrong tool selected) | Distinctive visual modes, undo |
| LTM (Semantic) | Retrieval failure | User cannot recall command syntax | Menus/icons (recognition not recall) |
| LTM (Procedural) | Skill decay | Eroded typing speed after 6 months | Spaced-retraining prompts |
| Episodic Buffer | Integration failure | User cannot link new feature to prior workflow | Onboarding tutorials, contextual help |
| Central Executive | Attentional blink | Critical alarm missed during menu navigation | Single-task modal dialogs |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Predicting WM Load for a Cockpit Display

**Problem:** A proposed avionics HMI shows 9 warning lights on a single 5-inch screen, each with a 2-word label. The pilot must, within 30 s, identify which light corresponds to "hydraulic pressure low" and act on it. Evaluate against WM constraints.

**Step 1 — Count chunks.**
Each light = 1 visual chunk. 9 lights ⇒ **9 chunks** to scan.

**Step 2 — Apply Cowan's revised capacity.**
$C_{WM} = 4 \pm 1$. Since $9 \gg 5$, the display **exceeds WM capacity** by ~ 80%.

**Step 3 — Apply decay function.**
Scan time ≈ 30 s. After 30 s of unrehearsed scan:

$$
R(30) = R_0 \cdot e^{-30/18} = R_0 \cdot e^{-1.667} \approx 0.189 \, R_0
$$

Only ~19% of the original trace strength remains by the time the pilot is supposed to act.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
The display is **non-compliant** with both capacity and decay constraints. Recommended redesign:
- Group the 9 lights into **3 logical clusters** of 3 (e.g., engine, hydraulic, electrical).
- Use **color + shape + position** redundancy (dual-coding).
- Provide a **persistent master caution** cue that primes the relevant cluster.

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark KTU answers:** Always end memory-analysis problems with a **specific redesign recommendation** grounded in (a) a model component, (b) a numerical benchmark, and (c) a recognized heuristic. This three-part closure is the KTU examiners' "complete answer" signature.

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Memory System (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the canonical multi-store memory architecture with Baddeley's working memory decomposition and the principal information pathways. It is designed to substitute for the textbook anatomical schematic while remaining renderable in any Mermaid-compatible viewer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[VisualStimulus] --> node2[IconicStore]
    node3[AuditoryStimulus] --> node4[EchoicStore]
    node5[HapticStimulus] --> node6[TactileStore]
    node2 --> node7{AttentionGate}
    node4 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[CentralExecutive]
    node8 --> node9[PhonologicalLoop]
    node8 --> node10[VisuospatialSketchpad]
    node8 --> node11[EpisodicBuffer]
    node9 --> node12[STMBuffer]
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13{RehearsalGate}
    node13 -->|Elaborative| node14[LTMSemantic]
    node13 -->|Repetitive| node15[LTMProcedural]
    node13 -->|Episodic| node16[LTMEpisodic]
    node12 --> node17[RetrievalCue]
    node14 --> node17
    node15 --> node17
    node16 --> node17
    node17 --> node8
    classDef store fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#0a2a66
    classDef gate fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#664d03
    classDef ltm fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0a3622
    classDef stim fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#58151c
    class node1,node3,node5 stim
    class node2,node4,node6,node9,node10,node11,node12 store
    class node7,node8,node13,node17 gate
    class node14,node15,node16 ltm
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU short-answer cue):**
- The three sensory registers on the left are **parallel and pre-attentive**.
- The single **AttentionGate** between sensory and central stages is the **bottleneck** that determines what enters awareness.
- The **CentralExecutive** in Baddeley's model is depicted as a gate, consistent with its role as an attentional controller rather than a storage.
- All three working-memory subcomponents (phonological, visuospatial, episodic) project into the **STMBuffer** before rehearsal routes them to one of the three LTM sub-stores.
- The **RetrievalCue** arrow returns from LTM back to the CentralExecutive, completing the **closed-loop cognitive cycle** essential for continuous HMI interaction.

---

### 4.2 Memory Process Lifecycle — Encoding, Storage, Retrieval

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PerceptualInput] --> nodeB[EncodingShallow]
    nodeA --> nodeC[EncodingDeep]
    nodeB --> nodeD[STMTrace]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE{RehearsalType}
    nodeE -->|Maintenance| nodeF[STMRetention]
    nodeE -->|Elaborative| nodeG[LTMSemanticTrace]
    nodeE -->|ProceduralPractice| nodeH[LTMProceduralTrace]
    nodeG --> nodeI{RetrievalMode}
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeI -->|Recall| nodeJ[RecallOutput]
    nodeI -->|Recognition| nodeK[RecognitionOutput]
    nodeI -->|Reconstruction| nodeL[ReconstructionOutput]
    classDef shallow fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b02a37
    classDef deep fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#1e7a1e
    classDef trace fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca
    classDef out fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404
    class nodeB shallow
    class nodeC deep
    class nodeD,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH trace
    class nodeJ,nodeK,nodeL out
```

**Interpretation Notes:**
- Shallow encoding (orthographic, surface features) → fragile STM trace, high decay.
- Deep encoding (semantic, elaborative) → strong LTM trace, low decay, high retrieval success.
- The three retrieval modes map directly to the **error-proneness** of HMI tasks: recognition is the most accurate, recall the least.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
*Distinguish between Sensory Memory and Short-Term Memory with respect to duration, capacity, and encoding format.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme cue:** 1 mark per distinguishing attribute + 1 mark for the encoding format.

| Attribute | Sensory Memory | Short-Term Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | **< 1 second** (iconic 0.25–0.5 s, echoic 3–4 s) | **15–30 seconds** (without rehearsal) |
| Capacity | Large (raw sensory buffer) | **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller) / **4 ± 1** (Cowan) |
| Encoding | **Modality-specific** (visual, auditory, haptic traces) | Primarily **phonological** (verbal) and **visuospatial** |
| Control | Pre-attentive | Requires **active attention** |
| Output | Direct projection to WM if attended | Conscious manipulation |

**Closing sentence (mandatory for full marks):** *Sensory memory acts as a high-bandwidth pre-attentive buffer, whereas STM is the capacity-limited conscious workbench where information is actively processed before transfer to LTM.*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
*Explain Baddeley's Episodic Buffer and its role in integrating information across memory subsystems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Episodic Buffer**, added by Baddeley & Hitch in **2000**, is a fourth component of working memory that serves as a **limited-capacity (≈ 4 chunks) integrative store**. It performs three essential functions:

1. **Integration** — binds information from the **Phonological Loop**, the **Visuospatial Sketchpad**, and **Long-Term Memory** into a coherent unified episode.
2. **Temporal Ordering** — preserves the sequence of events, supporting causal reasoning.
3. **Interface with LTM** — provides the bridge through which conscious WM contents acquire meaning by linking to pre-existing semantic knowledge.

**HMI Implication (closing line):** In interface design, the Episodic Buffer explains why **cross-modal redundancy** (a sound paired with its visual icon) is remembered far better than a single-modality cue — the buffer binds both into one episode that is then more durably encoded into LTM.

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Each Module carries one full 14-mark question with internal choice. Question A and Question B below are two **independent, mutually exclusive** options. The student answers **either** A or B. Each part-(a) carries 7 marks and part-(b) carries 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Multi-Store Model & Working Memory Deep Dive*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model** of memory with a neatly labeled diagram. Explain the role of **attention** and **rehearsal** in transferring information across stores.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **Baddeley's Working Memory Model**, explain how the components interact during a typical aircraft-pilot task involving auditory communication, a visual flight display, and recall of a memorized checklist. Justify which component is most likely to fail under high workload.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The **Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model** (1968) proposes three structurally and functionally distinct memory stores connected by control processes.

1. **Sensory Memory Registers** (1 mark)
   - Iconic (visual, ≈ 0.25–0.5 s), Echoic (auditory, ≈ 3–4 s), Haptic (≈ 2 s).
   - These are **modality-specific pre-attentive buffers** that hold raw physical traces of the input environment.

2. **Short-Term Memory (STM)** (1 mark)
   - Capacity **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956).
   - Duration **15–30 s** without rehearsal.
   - Encoded primarily in **phonological** form.
   - Rehearsal refreshes the trace approximately every **2 s**.

3. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** (1 mark)
   - Capacity **unlimited**; duration **minutes to lifetime**.
   - Encoded in **semantic** form.
   - Two principal sub-types: **declarative** (episodic + semantic) and **non-declarative** (procedural + priming + conditioning).

4. **Control Processes — Attention and Rehearsal** (2 marks)
   - **Attention** acts as the gateway from sensory registers to STM. Without attentional selection, sensory input decays within fractions of a second.
   - **Rehearsal** is of two types:
     - *Maintenance rehearsal* (silent repetition) — sustains items in STM but does not promote LTM transfer.
     - *Elaborative rehearsal* (linking to meaning, schemas) — promotes robust LTM encoding, the cognitive basis of learning.

5. **Serial vs Parallel Transfer** (1 mark)
   - The classical model assumes **serial transfer** (sensory → STM → LTM), but modern evidence (Baddeley; Craik) shows that **deep encoding can route information directly to LTM** without prolonged STM residence.

6. **Diagrammatic representation** (1 mark)
   - See **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram. The student should reproduce a similar three-box flow with bidirectional retrieval arrows and labeled attention/rehearsal gates.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner cue:** Award full 7 marks only if the student *distinguishes* the two rehearsal types and *labels the attention gate* explicitly on the diagram. A mere list of three stores earns at most 4 marks.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The pilot scenario decomposes naturally into Baddeley's components:

1. **Phonological Loop — Auditory ATC Communication** (1.5 marks)
   - The pilot listens to "Tower, descend four thousand, QNH 1013" while simultaneously displaying the spoken digits in WM.
   - The **Phonological Store** holds the raw acoustic trace; the **Articulatory Rehearsal Process** silently repeats the altitude and pressure values to prevent decay.
   - **Failure risk:** Echoic buffer in the ear fades within 3–4 s, and if rehearsal is interrupted by another auditory input, the digits are lost — a known contributor to **hearback errors** in aviation.

2. **Visuospatial Sketchpad — Visual Flight Display** (1.5 marks)
   - The Primary Flight Display (PFD) presents an **artificial horizon, airspeed tape, altitude tape, heading indicator**. The pilot must hold these in spatial WM.
   - The **Inner Scribe** sequences the scan pattern; the **Visual Cache** retains form and colour.
   - **Failure risk:** Display clutter forces the pilot to chunk more than **4 ± 1** visual items, violating Cowan's revised capacity. Critical symbology may be **saccadically skipped**.

3. **Central Executive — Checklist Recall + Task Switching** (1.5 marks)
   - The pilot must switch between ATC, PFD, ND, and a memorized **before-landing checklist** (gear, flaps, speed brake).
   - The Central Executive allocates attention and **inhibits irrelevant channels** (e.g., cabin announcements).
   - **Failure risk:** Under high workload, the central executive becomes the **bottleneck**. This is the **most likely component to fail**, leading to **checklist omission** (a known accident pattern in approach-and-landing incidents).

4. **Episodic Buffer — Integration of All Three** (1.5 marks)
   - Binds "descend 4000 ft" (phonological) with the *changing* altitude tape (visual) and the *memory* of the standard arrival procedure (LTM) into a single coherent episode.
   - **Failure risk:** Cross-modal binding errors (e.g., pilot hears "4000" but writes "400" on the scratchpad) — the **integration function** has failed.

5. **Synthesis — Most Likely Failure** (1 mark)
   - The **Central Executive** is the most likely to fail under high workload because it has no storage capacity of its own and must arbitrate between four simultaneously active channels. This is why **modern cockpit design** offloads checklist verification to **automated EICAS/FWS callouts** and **aural redundancies**, freeing the executive for cross-checking rather than rote memory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (marks deducted):** Students often attribute workload failure to STM as a whole. KTU 2024 scheme expects you to identify the **specific Baddeley sub-component**. Writing "WM fails" loses 2 marks; writing "the Central Executive fails due to attention-switching overload" gains full credit.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Memory Processes & HMI Design Application*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three memory processes** — encoding, storage, and retrieval — and discuss the **Levels-of-Processing** framework. How does depth of processing affect LTM retention?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medical-device startup is designing an infusion-pump interface for ICU nurses. Each shift, a nurse handles **~ 40 patients** and must program infusion rates under **time pressure and frequent interruptions**. Apply **memory theory** to propose **at least five (5) HMI design interventions** that reduce memory-related errors. Justify each intervention with a specific cognitive principle.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Encoding** (1.5 marks)
   - The transformation of perceptual input into a storable neural code.
   - **Acoustic encoding** (sound patterns), **Visual encoding** (images, shapes), **Semantic encoding** (meaning).
   - Encoding is **effortful**; depth depends on the cognitive resources allocated.

2. **Storage** (1.5 marks)
   - Maintenance of the encoded trace over time.
   - Affected by: (i) **Decay** (time-based weakening, exponential per Section 3.1), (ii) **Interference** — *proactive* (old learning disrupts new) and *retroactive* (new learning disrupts old), (iii) **Consolidation** (hippocampal-to-cortical transfer during sleep).

3. **Retrieval** (1.5 marks)
   - Reactivation of stored information.
   - Three modes: **Recall** (no cue, hardest — typing a password), **Recognition** (cue present, easiest — selecting a menu item), **Reconstruction** (fragments reassembled, error-prone).
   - Retrieval is **cue-dependent**; the **encoding specificity principle** (Tulving & Thomson, 1973) states that retrieval success is maximized when the cue matches the encoding context.

4. **Levels-of-Processing Framework (Craik & Lockhart, 1972)** (1.5 marks)
   - **Shallow processing** — structural, orthographic features (e.g., "this word is in capital letters"). Produces weak, decay-prone traces.
   - **Intermediate processing** — phonemic features (e.g., "this word rhymes with X"). Modest retention.
   - **Deep processing** — semantic, elaborative features (e.g., "this word means Y, and it relates to my prior experience with Z"). Produces strong, durable traces.
   - **Empirical evidence:** Hyde & Jenkins (1973) — semantic-orientation tasks produced 50% better free recall than orthographic-orientation tasks on identical word lists.

5. **Depth of Processing → LTM Retention** (1 mark)
   - Deeper processing generates **more retrieval routes** (rich semantic networks), so the trace is more easily cued and more resistant to interference and decay.
   - **HMI corollary:** Training programs that emphasize the **meaning** of a UI pattern (semantic) produce more durable operator skill than rote memorization (shallow) — supporting **scenario-based training** in safety-critical domains.

> [!TIP]
> **Closing line for full marks:** "Depth of processing is to memory what soil preparation is to agriculture — shallow sowing yields thin roots, deep sowing yields harvest that survives drought."

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks** (Five interventions, each ~1.4 marks)

| # | Intervention | Memory Principle | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drug name + dose displayed on confirmation screen**; nurse must tap "Confirm" (recognition not recall). | **Encoding Specificity & Recognition > Recall** | The on-screen cue matches the encoding context; recall error rate drops to ≈ 5% of pure-recall error (formula 10, Section 2.5). |
| 2 | **Color-code drug classes** (e.g., analgesics = blue, vasopressors = red) with **redundant shape coding** on syringe labels. | **Dual-Coding Hypothesis (Paivio)** + **Episodic Buffer integration** | Two sensory channels bind into a single, more durable LTM episode. |
| 3 | **Auto-population of patient ID and weight from EHR** — nurse does not re-enter. | **STM Offloading + Decay mitigation** | Removes 4–5 chunks from WM, keeping load within Cowan's 4 ± 1 ceiling. |
| 4 | **Persistent status banner** showing current infusion rate for **all 40 patients** on a single overview screen. | **LTM Decay function (Section 3.1)** | Persistent display neutralizes the 18 s STM decay; nurses do not have to remember rates across patients. |
| 5 | **Spaced, just-in-time micro-refreshers** at the start of each shift (3-min scenario quiz). | **Spacing Effect (Ebbinghaus) + Elaborative Rehearsal** | Distributes practice over time, boosts LTM retention, and uses deep semantic processing. |
| 6 *(Bonus)* | **Confirmation tone + visual flash** for every completed programming action. | **Redundant audio-visual encoding** + **Episodic binding** | Cross-modal redundancy strengthens the Episodic Buffer integration. |

**Synthesis closing paragraph (mandatory for full marks):**
The cumulative effect of these interventions is to **shift the cognitive load from the operator's limited WM to the machine's external memory**, a principle articulated by **Donald Norman's "knowledge in the world"** design heuristic. The infusion pump becomes a *cognitive prosthesis* that compensates for the very memory constraints (capacity, decay, interference) that the multi-store model predicts will otherwise lead to error.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (KTU 2024 pattern):** Many students list interventions without naming the cognitive principle. The marking scheme requires *each intervention to be paired with a named principle from memory theory*. An intervention without a principle earns at most 0.5 of the 1.4 marks allocated. Total marks for an unjustified answer are capped at **4/7** even if five interventions are listed.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory (Module 1, KTU 2024)**

- **Three-store model** = Sensory → STM → LTM; information flow controlled by **attention** and **rehearsal**.
- **Sensory memory durations:** iconic 0.25–0.5 s, echoic 3–4 s, haptic ~ 2 s.
- **STM capacity:** Miller's **7 ± 2**; Cowan's revised **4 ± 1** (use Cowan for 2024 scheme answers).
- **STM duration without rehearsal:** 15–30 s; **decay time constant $\tau \approx 18$ s**; **rehearsal refresh period $\approx 2$ s**.
- **Baddeley's WM** = Phonological Loop + Visuospatial Sketchpad + Episodic Buffer + Central Executive (no storage, only attention control).
- **LTM subdivisions:** Declarative (episodic + semantic) and Non-declarative (procedural + priming + conditioning).
- **Three processes:** Encoding, Storage, Retrieval (recall / recognition / reconstruction).
- **Levels-of-Processing:** Shallow (orthographic) → Deep (semantic). Deeper processing = stronger LTM trace.
- **Key formula:** $R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ for STM decay.
- **Key formula:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ for Fitts' Law (Shannon formulation, with the mandatory $+1$).
- **Design rule #1:** **Recognition > Recall** (Nielsen heuristic #6).
- **Design rule #2:** **Limit chunks to 4–5 per screen** (Cowan's capacity).
- **Design rule #3:** **Use persistent displays for critical info** (decay mitigation).
- **Design rule #4:** **Use redundant multi-modal encoding** (dual-coding → Episodic Buffer).
- **Design rule #5:** **Shift load to the machine** (Norman's "knowledge in the world").
- **Most common exam trap:** Confusing STM with WM, or attributing workload failure to "WM" without naming the **Central Executive** specifically.
- **Most common derivation trap:** Fitts' Law without the $+1$ inside the logarithm.
- **Most common HMI trap:** Listing design rules without grounding each in a named cognitive principle.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing and Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Processing and Networks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Processing in HMI Context — The Human as an Information Processor ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Advanced Human Machine Interfaces, **Processing** refers to the cognitive and perceptual mechanisms by which a human operator receives stimuli, encodes them, stores them, retrieves them, and generates responses to interact with a machine system. It is modelled formally through the **Human Information Processing (HIP)** paradigm, which treats the user as a biological information channel with finite capacity, latency, and error characteristics analogous to a digital communication system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Processing is the bridge between *perception* (input) and *motor action* (output). In any HMI design, the human is never assumed to be instantaneous; designers must budget for **reaction time, memory decay, attention switching, and cognitive load**. ### The Human Processor Model (Card, Moran, Newell — Keystroke-Level Model, KLM Foundation) The classical **Model Human Processor (MHP)** decomposes cognition into three interacting subsystems: | Subsystem | Functional Role | Corresponds To | |---|---|---| | **Perceptual Processor** | Translates raw sensory stimuli into internal cognitive representations | Eyes, ears, proprioceptors | | **Cognitive Processor** | Manipulates symbols, applies rules, makes decisions | Working memory, long-term memory access | | **Motor Processor** | Executes physical actions (keystrokes, mouse movements, gestures) | Hands, fingers, vocal cords | ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Office Receptionist" Imagine a receptionist in a busy office. The **mail tray** (Sensory Memory) holds every letter that lands on the desk for a fraction of a second. The **active desk** (Short-Term / Working Memory) is where the receptionist opens and reads only the most urgent letters — it has a strict capacity of about 4–7 items. Important items are filed into the **archive room** (Long-Term Memory) where they may stay for years but require effort to retrieve. When a decision is made, a **handwritten reply** is dispatched (Motor Output). The receptionist cannot process 100 letters simultaneously — there is a *bottleneck* (the bottleneck of attention) just like the human cognitive system. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box:** > - **Sensory Memory:** Capacity = very large; Duration ≈ **250–500 ms** (iconic for vision, echoic for audition). > - **Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory:** Capacity = **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law); Duration ≈ **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal. > - **Long-Term Memory (LTM):** Capacity = essentially **unlimited**; Duration = **minutes to a lifetime**. --- ## 1.2 Networks in HMI Context — Connecting Humans, Machines, and Data ### Formal Definition **Networks** in HMI refer to the communication infrastructure (LAN, WAN, internet, wireless, IoT fabric) that interconnects input devices, processing units, display systems, sensors, and remote users, enabling **distributed interaction**, real-time telemetry, and collaborative control. A modern HMI is rarely a standalone device — it is a **node in a network of nodes**. ### Key Network Topologies in HMI | Topology | Description | HMI Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Client–Server** | Centralized server hosts logic/data; clients send requests | Web dashboards, SCADA HMIs | | **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** | All nodes equal; resources shared directly | Collaborative design tools, multiplayer interfaces | | **Publish–Subscribe** | Producers push events to a broker; consumers subscribe | Real-time IoT sensor streams in industrial HMI | | **Hierarchical / Edge–Fog–Cloud** | Processing distributed across tiers | Latency-critical HMI (AR/VR, autonomous vehicles) | ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Control Tower" The **airport control tower** is the network hub. Pilots (users) communicate via radio (network protocol), controllers (servers) coordinate takeoffs/landings, and radar feeds (sensors) deliver real-time data. The tower cannot directly see every plane — it relies on a *distributed stream* of information. If the radio link drops, **graceful degradation** (fallback procedures) is essential. This is precisely how networked HMI handles **latency, packet loss, and disconnects**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Constants & Metrics (Bold per KTU convention):** > - **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Typical LAN = **< 1 ms**; Internet = **20–200 ms**; Satellite = **500–800 ms**. > - **Human-Relevant Latency Thresholds:** < **100 ms** feels instantaneous; **100–300 ms** is noticeable; > **1 s** interrupts flow. > - **Bandwidth:** measured in **bps, kbps, Mbps, Gbps**. > - **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** BDP = Bandwidth × RTT — determines in-flight data for full-duplex links. > [!NOTE] > **Why Networks Matter in HMI Design:** > 1. They determine **response latency** (user satisfaction). > 2. They govern **data freshness** (critical in medical, aviation, industrial control). > 3. They define **scalability** of the interface (1 user vs. 10,000 users). > 4. They introduce **failure modes** (disconnection, jitter, packet loss) that the HMI must surface gracefully. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Deep Dive — The Human Information Processing Pipeline The human processor is conceptualized as a **serial bottleneck with parallel pre-processing**. Below is the step-by-step flow: ### Step 1: Stimulus Reception (Sensory Register) - Raw physical energy (light, sound, pressure) hits sensory receptors. - Information is held briefly in a modality-specific buffer. - **Duration:** ~250 ms (visual iconic memory), ~2–4 seconds (auditory echoic memory). - **Capacity:** Extremely high (essentially all incoming stimuli). ### Step 2: Pattern Recognition & Attention Filter - Pre-attentive features (color, orientation, motion) are processed in parallel by the **perceptual processor**. - Selective attention acts as a **filter** (Broadbent, 1958) or **attenuator** (Treisman, 1960), allowing only relevant stimuli into working memory. - **Why it matters in HMI:** Designers must exploit pre-attentive features (e.g., red for danger) to bypass cognitive load. ### Step 3: Working Memory (Short-Term Memory) Processing - The "scratchpad" of the mind — where conscious reasoning occurs. - **Capacity:** **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956). A "chunk" is a meaningful unit, not a single datum. - **Duration:** ~15–30 s without rehearsal; rehearsal refreshes the buffer. - **Operations:** Encoding, comparison, transformation, retrieval from LTM. ### Step 4: Long-Term Memory (LTM) Retrieval - LTM is the permanent store: episodic, semantic, procedural. - **Access Time:** ~100–400 ms for retrieval (Fitts & Posner stages: cognitive → associative → autonomous). - **Organization:** Schemas, scripts, mental models. - **Why it matters in HMI:** Interfaces should align with users' pre-existing mental models to reduce learning time. ### Step 5: Decision & Response Selection - The cognitive processor selects an action based on rules, goals, and retrieved knowledge. - **Hick's Law** governs choice time: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$, where $n$ is the number of choices. ### Step 6: Motor Execution - The motor processor issues commands to effectors (hands, voice, eyes). - **Fitts's Law** predicts movement time: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$, where $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width. ### Step 7: Feedback Loop - The output produces a result the user perceives, closing the loop. This is the basis of **Norman’s Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation** (1988). --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Memorize units and boundary conditions.** | # | Law / Model | Formula | Variables & Units | Application in HMI | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hick's Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = reaction time (s); $n$ = number of equally likely choices; $a, b$ = empirically fitted constants | Menu design, button grouping | | 2 | **Fitts's Law** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | $MT$ = movement time (s); $D$ = distance to target (m); $W$ = target width (m); $a, b$ = empirical constants | Pointer/touch target sizing, button placement | | 3 | **Miller's Law** | $STM_{capacity} = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | — | Chunking info, limiting menu items | | 4 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{p}$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude; $I$ = stimulus intensity; $k$ = constant; $p$ = modality-specific exponent (vision ≈ 0.33, brightness ≈ 0.5, heaviness ≈ 1.45) | Visual scaling, haptic design | | 5 | **Response Time (Human Processor)** | $T_{total} \approx T_{perc} + T_{cog} + T_{mot}$ | All in ms. Typical: perception ≈ **100 ms**, cognition ≈ **70 ms**, motor ≈ **70 ms** | Total interaction budget | | 6 | **Network Throughput (Shannon Capacity)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = channel capacity (bps); $B$ = bandwidth (Hz); $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (unitless) | Theoretical max data rate | | 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = BW \times RTT$ | $BW$ in bits/s; $RTT$ in s; $BDP$ in bits | Window sizing for streaming HMI | | 8 | **Queuing Delay (Little's Law)** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = avg items in system; $\lambda$ = arrival rate; $W$ = avg wait time | Server load, input lag in HMI | | 9 | **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** | $T_{execute} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$ | $T_K$ = keystroke; $T_P$ = pointing; $T_H$ = homing; $T_D$ = drawing; $T_M$ = mental prep; $T_R$ = system response | Predictive task time estimation | | 10 | **Nyquist Sampling** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sample rate; $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | Audio/video streaming fidelity | --- ## 2.3 Deep Dive — Networked HMI Architectures ### The Three-Tier Client–Server HMI 1. **Presentation Tier (Client UI):** Renders the interface (browser, mobile app, kiosk). Concerned only with input capture and display output. 2. **Application Tier (Logic Server):** Processes business rules, validates inputs, mediates between UI and data. 3. **Data Tier (Database / Sensor Hub):** Stores persistent state, ingests sensor streams. > **Real-world utility:** Industrial SCADA systems, hospital information systems, smart-building dashboards — all use this tiered model to separate concerns and allow independent scaling. ### The Pub/Sub (Publish–Subscribe) Model in Modern HMI - **Publishers** (sensors, devices) push events tagged by topic to a **message broker** (e.g., MQTT broker, Kafka). - **Subscribers** (HMIs, dashboards, control algorithms) register interest in topics and receive updates. - **Advantages:** Decoupling, scalability, real-time push (no polling), low bandwidth. - **Example:** A factory floor with 1,000 temperature sensors streaming to a centralized HMI over MQTT — a polling model would be infeasible. ### Edge–Fog–Cloud Hierarchy in Latency-Sensitive HMI | Tier | Location | Latency | Role | |---|---|---|---| | **Edge** | At the device/sensor | < **1 ms** | Time-critical control, safety interlocks | | **Fog** | Local gateway | **1–10 ms** | Local aggregation, preprocessing | | **Cloud** | Remote data center | **20–200 ms** | Heavy analytics, ML, long-term storage | > **Real-world utility:** Autonomous vehicles use edge processing for collision avoidance (cannot wait for cloud round-trip), fog for fleet coordination, cloud for HD-map updates and training. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Fitts's Law from Information-Theoretic Principles Fitts's Law is derived by modelling the pointing act as a noisy communication channel. Below is the complete, step-by-step derivation that KTU examiners may expect for a 7-mark sub-question. ### Step 1: Define the Task as a Signal Transmission A user moves a pointer from a starting position toward a target of width $W$ located at distance $D$. The user's motor system must transmit enough information to specify the target's location accurately despite **motor noise** (variance in endpoint position increases with movement amplitude). ### Step 2: Quantify Task Difficulty (Index of Difficulty) The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits is defined as the logarithm of the ratio of target distance to target width: $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ > **Reasoning (text row):** We add **1** to the ratio to ensure the logarithm is well-defined when $D$ is very small relative to $W$ (the task becomes trivially easy, $ID \to 0$). ### Step 3: Apply Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem By analogy with Shannon's theorem, the **minimum movement time (MT)** is proportional to the difficulty divided by the channel throughput (motor bandwidth): $$ MT = \dfrac{ID}{C_{motor}} = \dfrac{\log_2(D/W + 1)}{C_{motor}} $$ > **Reasoning:** A more difficult task (larger $D$, smaller $W$) requires more "motor bits" and hence more time. ### Step 4: Introduce Empirical Linear Correction Empirically, the model is fitted with intercept $a$ and slope $b = 1/C_{motor}$: $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) $$ > **Reasoning:** The intercept $a$ accounts for reaction/initiation time; the slope $b$ captures the inverse of motor channel capacity. Typical desktop values: $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 100$ ms/bit. ### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example Suppose a button is **160 px wide** and located **320 px away** from the cursor. Find the movement time. **Given:** - $D = 320$ px - $W = 160$ px - $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s/bit **Computation:** $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{320}{160} + 1\right) = \log_2(2 + 1) = \log_2(3) $$ $$ \log_2(3) = \dfrac{\ln 3}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{1.0986}{0.6931} \approx 1.585 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 1.585 = 0.05 + 0.1585 = 0.2085 \text{ s} $$ > **Conclusion:** The user will take approximately **209 ms** to acquire the target. HMI designers use this to optimize button layouts. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Hick's Law with Hysteresis Correction ### Step 1: Hick's Original Formulation Hick measured that reaction time increases **logarithmically** with the number of choices: $$ T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) $$ > **Reasoning:** Each additional choice adds one bit of decision information; $b$ is the per-bit decision time (≈ 100–200 ms/bit for humans). ### Step 2: Hyman Correction (1953) Hyman refined the formula by removing the "+1" and using a base that depends on stimulus uncertainty: $$ T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n) $$ > **Reasoning:** When $n \geq 2$, the "+1" is empirically negligible. Hyman showed that the formula applies cleanly for $n$ between 2 and 10. ### Step 3: Worked Example — Menu Design A dropdown menu has **8 items** ($n = 8$). Compute decision time using $a = 0.15$ s, $b = 0.20$ s/bit. $$ T = 0.15 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2(8+1) = 0.15 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2(9) $$ $$ \log_2(9) = \dfrac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.170 \text{ bits} $$ $$ T = 0.15 + 0.20 \times 3.170 = 0.15 + 0.634 = 0.784 \text{ s} $$ > **HMI design takeaway:** If the menu is reduced to 4 items: $$ T = 0.15 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2(5) = 0.15 + 0.20 \times 2.322 = 0.15 + 0.464 = 0.614 \text{ s} $$ > **Time saved: ≈ 170 ms per selection** — substantial across many interactions. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Shannon Capacity for Networked HMI ### Step 1: State Shannon's Hartley Theorem For a band-limited additive white Gaussian noise channel, the maximum reliable data rate is: $$ C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) $$ > **Reasoning:** Higher bandwidth and stronger signal-to-noise ratio increase capacity. ### Step 2: Worked Example — Wi-Fi HMI Video Stream A factory HMI streams a 1080p camera feed over a **20 MHz** Wi-Fi channel with **S/N = 1000** (30 dB). **Given:** - $B = 20 \times 10^6$ Hz - $S/N = 1000$ **Computation:** $$ 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} = 1 + 1000 = 1001 $$ $$ \log_2(1001) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/s/Hz} $$ $$ C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 9.967 = 1.993 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} \approx 199.3 \text{ Mbps} $$ > **Conclusion:** Theoretical max is ~199 Mbps. A 1080p H.264 stream needs ~6 Mbps — comfortably within capacity. However, this is the **theoretical** limit; real throughput is **30–50%** of this due to protocol overhead. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Processing Models vs Network Models | Dimension | Human Processing (HIP) | Network Processing | |---|---|---| | **Bottleneck** | Working memory capacity | Bandwidth / RTT | | **Throughput metric** | Bits/sec in cognition (≈ 50 bits/s) | Shannon capacity (Mbps–Gbps) | | **Error mode** | Forgetting, attention lapse | Packet loss, jitter, corruption | | **Storage** | LTM (unlimited but slow) | Database / cloud (unlimited, fast) | | **Latency** | 200–500 ms typical response | 1–200 ms typical | | **Scaling law** | Logarithmic (Hick, Fitts) | Logarithmic (Shannon) | | **Failure recovery** | Rehearsal, schema activation | Retransmission (TCP), FEC | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Human Information Processing Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stimulus
Light Sound Pressure] --> B[Sensory Memory
Iconic Echoic
250ms to 4s] B --> C[Attention Filter
Broadbent Treisman] C --> D[Working Memory
7 plus minus 2 chunks
15 to 30s] D --> E[Long Term Memory
Schemas Scripts
Unlimited capacity] E --> D D --> F[Decision Stage
Hicks Law] F --> G[Motor Processor
Fitts Law] G --> H[Action
Keystroke Click Voice] H --> I[Feedback
Display Audio Haptic] I --> A ``` ## 4.2 Three-Tier Client-Server HMI Network (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ClientTier[Presentation Tier] UI1[Web Browser HMI] UI2[Mobile App] UI3[Kiosk Display] end subgraph AppTier[Application Tier] APIGW[API Gateway] AUTH[Auth Service] LOGIC[Business Logic Engine] end subgraph DataTier[Data Tier] DB[(Relational DB)] SENSOR[Sensor Hub] CACHE[(Redis Cache)] end UI1 --> APIGW UI2 --> APIGW UI3 --> APIGW APIGW --> AUTH APIGW --> LOGIC LOGIC --> DB LOGIC --> SENSOR LOGIC --> CACHE ``` ## 4.3 Edge-Fog-Cloud Hierarchy for Latency-Critical HMI ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EdgeTier[Edge Tier Latency under 1ms] E1[Safety Interlock] E2[Motor Controller] E3[Local UI Display] end subgraph FogTier[Fog Tier 1 to 10ms] F1[Local Aggregator] F2[Preprocessor] F3[Local Alarm Engine] end subgraph CloudTier[Cloud Tier 20 to 200ms] C1[Big Data Analytics] C2[ML Training Pipeline] C3[Long Term Storage] end E1 --> F1 E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 F1 --> F2 F2 --> F3 F3 --> C1 F3 --> C2 F3 --> C3 ``` ## 4.4 Pub Sub Message Flow for IoT-Driven HMI ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Temperature Sensor] -->|publish topic temp| MB[MQTT Broker] S2[Pressure Sensor] -->|publish topic pressure| MB S3[Vibration Sensor] -->|publish topic vibration| MB MB -->|subscribe temp| HMI1[Operator Dashboard] MB -->|subscribe pressure| HMI1 MB -->|subscribe vibration| HMI1 MB -->|subscribe temp| CTRL[Auto Control Loop] MB -->|subscribe all| LOG[Cloud Logger] ``` ## 4.5 Fitts Law Acquisition Geometry (Mermaid Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart LR START[Pointer Start Position] -->|Distance D| TARGET[Target Width W] TARGET --> ID[Index of Difficulty
log2 D divided by W plus 1] ID --> MT[Movement Time
a plus b times ID] MT --> FEED[Visual Feedback] FEED --> START ``` ## 4.6 Combined HMI Loop — Human + Network Interaction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HumanSide[Human Side] H1[Eyes Ears Skin] H2[Perceptual Processor] H3[Working Memory] H4[Motor Processor] H5[Hands Voice] end subgraph NetworkSide[Network Side] N1[Input Device] N2[Protocol Stack TCP UDP MQTT] N3[Application Server] N4[Display Renderer] end H1 -->|Stimulus| H2 H2 --> H3 H3 --> H4 H4 --> H5 H5 -->|Click Gesture| N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 N3 --> N4 N4 -->|Visual Audio Haptic| H1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **Define the Human Information Processing (HIP) model. List its three core subsystems with their typical latencies.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Human Information Processing (HIP) model (Card, Moran & Newell) treats the human operator as a biological information-processing system composed of three interacting subsystems: 1. **Perceptual Processor** — converts raw sensory input (light, sound) into internal cognitive codes. Typical latency ≈ **100 ms**. 2. **Cognitive Processor** — performs working-memory operations, decision-making, and LTM retrieval. Typical latency ≈ **70 ms**. 3. **Motor Processor** — translates decisions into physical actions. Typical latency ≈ **70 ms**. Total reaction time budget ≈ 100 + 70 + 70 = **240 ms** for a simple choice reaction. > **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing the three subsystems, 1 mark for correct latencies. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **Differentiate between Client–Server and Publish–Subscribe architectures. Give one HMI use case for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Client–Server | Publish–Subscribe | |---|---|---| | **Coupling** | Tight — client knows server address | Loose — publisher and subscriber are decoupled by broker | | **Communication** | Request–response (pull) | Event-driven (push) | | **Latency** | Depends on polling interval | Near real-time | | **HMI Use Case** | Hospital Information System where the dashboard requests patient records on demand (1) | Factory IoT HMI where 1,000 sensors push temperature updates to a central dashboard via MQTT (2) | > **Mark distribution:** 1 mark per difference (any two), 1 mark for valid use cases. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **(a)** Explain Fitts's Law in detail. Derive the Index of Difficulty (ID) and the Movement Time (MT) equation from first principles. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A touch target on a mobile HMI is **40 px wide** and located **120 px** from the user's thumb start position. Using Fitts's Law with empirical constants $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit, calculate the predicted movement time. Comment on whether the target size is appropriate given KTU HMI guidelines. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Fitts's Law — Information-Theoretic Foundation:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Fitts framed the pointing task as transmission of information over a noisy motor channel. The user must resolve target ambiguity in the presence of motor noise whose standard deviation scales with movement amplitude. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Define **Index of Difficulty (ID)** as the log of target distance to width ratio, in bits: $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ The "+1" is added to keep the logarithm well-defined and non-negative. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply Shannon's channel capacity analogy. The minimum movement time is proportional to the ID divided by motor channel capacity $C_m$: $$ MT_{min} = \dfrac{ID}{C_m} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Introduce empirical linear correction to account for reaction/initiation time and device-specific delays: $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ where $a$ is the intercept (initiation time, typical 30–100 ms) and $b$ is the slope (≈ 100–200 ms/bit). The law implies that **larger targets and shorter distances reduce movement time logarithmically** — the foundation of touch-target sizing guidelines (Apple's 44 pt, Material's 48 dp). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $D = 120$ px - $W = 40$ px - $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s/bit **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the ratio: $$ \dfrac{D}{W} + 1 = \dfrac{120}{40} + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute ID in bits: $$ ID = \log_2(4) = 2.0 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute MT: $$ MT = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 2.0 = 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.25 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Comment:** The 250 ms movement time is acceptable, but KTU HMI guidelines (aligned with WCAG 2.5.5 Target Size — Level AAA) recommend a minimum target size of **44 × 44 CSS pixels** and ID ≤ 4 bits for primary actions. The current target yields ID = 2 bits, which is well within bounds. However, on small mobile screens the same pixel size may correspond to a smaller *visual* size; designers should verify on-device. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style] (Internal Choice) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Human Information Processing (HIP) model**. Explain the role of Sensory Memory, Short-Term Memory, and Long-Term Memory in HMI design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A networked HMI dashboard streams data over a **10 Mbps** link with RTT of **80 ms**. Compute the **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** and explain its significance for designing the receive window of the streaming client. If the protocol uses TCP with default 64 KB window, will it saturate the link? Show calculations. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The HIP pipeline: - **Sensory Memory** → raw stimulus buffer (iconic/echoic). - **Attention Filter** → selective routing (Broadbent/Treisman). - **Working/Short-Term Memory** → conscious workspace (7 ± 2 chunks, Miller). - **Long-Term Memory** → permanent store of schemas, scripts, procedural skills. - **Motor Processor** → action output. - **Feedback** loop closes to perception. **Roles in HMI design (4 marks — 1.33 each):** 1. **Sensory Memory role (1 mark):** Designers must ensure stimuli are **above threshold** (visible/audible) for at least 250–500 ms to be registered. Examples: flash duration for warnings, button hover states lasting > 200 ms. 2. **Short-Term Memory role (1 mark):** Since STM holds only 7 ± 2 chunks, **chunking** and **progressive disclosure** are critical. Phone numbers, URLs, menu items should respect this limit. 3. **Long-Term Memory role (1 mark):** Interfaces should align with users' **pre-existing schemas** (e.g., a "trash can" icon for delete). Affordances, recognizability over recall, and consistency exploit LTM to reduce cognitive load. 4. **Integration (1 mark):** A well-designed HMI minimizes **gulfs of execution and evaluation** (Norman, 1988) by mapping user intentions to system actions (low execution gulf) and system states to user perception (low evaluation gulf). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $BW = 10 \text{ Mbps} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$ - $RTT = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.080 \text{ s}$ **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute BDP: $$ BDP = BW \times RTT = 10 \times 10^6 \times 0.080 = 800{,}000 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Convert to kilobytes: $$ BDP = \dfrac{800{,}000}{8 \times 1024} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{8192} \approx 97.66 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compare to TCP default 64 KB window: $$ \text{Window}_{TCP} = 64 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits} $$ Since $524{,}288 \text{ bits} < 800{,}000 \text{ bits}$, the **default 64 KB window is insufficient** to keep the pipe full. **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Conclusion & significance:** The TCP sender must wait for an ACK before sending more data, and with a 64 KB window, only 64 KB can be in flight at a time. Throughput will be capped at: $$ \text{Max throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Window}}{RTT} = \dfrac{524{,}288 \text{ bits}}{0.080 \text{ s}} = 6{,}553{,}600 \text{ bps} \approx 6.55 \text{ Mbps} $$ So the link will be **utilized at only ~65%**. To saturate the 10 Mbps link, the TCP window must be increased to at least: $$ \text{Window}_{min} = BW \times RTT = 800{,}000 \text{ bits} = 100 \text{ KB} $$ > **HMI implication:** Streaming dashboards, real-time video, and sensor UIs must negotiate adequate socket buffers and TCP window scaling to avoid jitter and stalls. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Units confusion:** Mbps vs MB/s vs Kbps — always write units explicitly. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for ambiguous units. > 2. **Base of logarithm:** Hick's/Fitts's law uses $\log_2$ (bits), NOT natural log or base-10. Mixing bases = 0 marks for the formula. > 3. **Fitts's "+1":** Many students forget the +1. Examiners explicitly check this in derivations. > 4. **Sketching:** In HIP model questions, always draw the block diagram. A text-only answer loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Confusing Hick's with Fitts's:** Hick's applies to *decision* time (number of choices); Fitts's applies to *movement* time (distance/size). Examiners frequently test this distinction. > 6. **Latency vs Throughput:** BDP is *capacity in flight* (bits), not throughput (bps). Do not interchange. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Human Processor Pipeline:** Stimulus → Sensory Memory → Attention → Working Memory → LTM → Decision → Motor → Action → Feedback. - [ ] **Miller's Law:** STM capacity = **7 ± 2 chunks**;
