4. **Conclude** with the trade-off: higher development cost is *amortised* across millions of users, hence the paradigm's dominance. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *Compare Menu-Driven and Natural-Language paradigms across the five HCI quality lenses: Affordance, Feedback Latency, Error Recovery, Learnability, Accessibility.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A rural health-worker in a low-bandwidth region needs a system to record patient vitals. Recommend a paradigm and defend your choice, ruling out at least two alternatives.* **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

Produce a **5-row comparison table** (one row per lens) with verdict on *which* paradigm wins *which* lens, plus a one-line justification per cell. *(5 × 1 = 5 marks)* Conclude that *Menu* wins on error recovery and learnability; *NL* wins on accessibility. *(2 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

1. **Recommend:** *Multimodal* (voice-in + icon-tap + local-first) or *Form-Based* with audio prompts. *(1 mark)*
2. **Defend with the five lenses:** high learnability, high error recovery, high accessibility (audio for low-literacy). *(2 marks)*
3. **Rule out CLI:** literacy / syntax barrier. *(1 mark)*
4. **Rule out pure NL:** unreliable on low-bandwidth NLU pipelines, requires strong server. *(1 mark)*
5. **Mention** that *low bandwidth* forces *local-first multimodal* design. *(1 mark)*
6. **Conclude** with one engineering trade-off (e.g., battery cost of always-on microphone). *(1 mark)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Paradigms" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *paradigm* with *device*.** Saying *"touchscreen is a paradigm"* costs you 1 mark — touchscreen is an *input device*, WIMP is the paradigm.
> 2. **Forgetting to give an example.** A definition *without* an example is half-marks at best.
> 3. **Not ruling out alternatives.** In a 7-mark "justify" sub-part, the absence of *at least two ruled-out alternatives* costs you 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Direct Manipulation and GUI.** GUI is the *technology*; Direct Manipulation is the *paradigm*.
> 5. **Ignoring the cognitive-load lens.** Examiners explicitly map questions to CO1 + CO2 of the syllabus — *cognitive load* and *learnability* are mandatory vocabulary.
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion.** A 14-mark answer without a *defending conclusion* loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Writing "WIMP" without expanding it once** — examiners check the abbreviation; spell it out at first use.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔁 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam Entry:**

- ✅ **Definition first:** A paradigm is a *model of interaction*, not a device or technology.
- ✅ **Seven canonical paradigms** (in KTU syllabus order): Batch → CLI → Menu/Form → WIMP/DM → Natural Language → Multimodal → VR/Immersive/Tangible.
- ✅ **Expand every acronym at first use:** WIMP, NLU, BCI, 6DoF.
- ✅ **Three properties of Direct Manipulation** (Hutchins et al., 1985): *continuous representation, physical actions / button presses, rapid reversible operations with immediate visible impact*.
- ✅ **Five HCI quality lenses:** Affordance, Feedback Latency, Error Recovery, Learnability, Accessibility.
- ✅ **Four-cost decomposition** (for cognitive-load arguments): Syntax + Memory + Error + Feedback.
- ✅ **Multimodal** = *Fusion* (many inputs → one intent) + *Fission* (one result → many outputs).
- ✅ **VR/AR ≠ GUI**: immersive paradigm has *proprioceptive* feedback, not symbolic.
- ✅ **Natural Language** has *lowest user cost* but *highest system cost* — be ready to mention this asymmetry.
- ✅ **Always give a real-world example** for any paradigm you name.
- ✅ **Rule out two alternatives** in any "justify" sub-part.
- ✅ **Conclude with a trade-off statement** — engineering is about trade-offs, not absolutes.
- ✅ **Cite Hutchins, Hollan & Norman (1985)** for Direct Manipulation — it signals to the examiner that you read the primary literature.
- ✅ **Memorise the comparison table** in §2.2 — it is the single most marks-efficient artefact.
- ✅ **Accessibility** is *not optional* in NEP 2020-aligned assessment — always mention it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mistakes Performed While Designing a Computer System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mistakes Performed While Designing a Computer System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI)** and **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, the term **"Mistakes Performed While Designing a Computer System"** refers to the systematic catalog of **human factors engineering errors, usability violations, and cognitive ergonomics failures** that designers commit during the conceptualization, prototyping, and deployment phases of an interactive computing system. These mistakes are studied under the umbrella of **User-Centered Design (UCD) violations** and are deeply rooted in the seminal works of **Donald A. Norman** (*"The Design of Everyday Things"*, 1988) and **Jakob Nielsen** (*"10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design"*, 1994), which constitute the foundational reference material for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2.

A **design mistake**, in HMI terminology, is any deliberate or inadvertent decision made by the system architect that creates a **gulf of execution** (the gap between the user's goal and the means to achieve it) or a **gulf of evaluation** (the gap between the system's physical state and the user's perception of it). These gulfs are the root cause of nearly every documented usability defect in interactive systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 learning outcome emphasizes that students must be able to **identify, classify, and propose remediation strategies** for at least **8 to 10 distinct categories** of design mistakes. The KTU board examiner frequently tests the ability to map a real-world UI defect (e.g., a poorly placed "Delete" button) to its underlying heuristic category.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car where the **accelerator and brake pedals are switched**. A person who has never driven before would press what they think is the brake, and the car would accelerate. This is **not the driver's mistake** — it is a **design mistake** by the car manufacturer. The pedals lack proper **signifiers** (visual cues), the **mapping** between the driver's mental model ("stop = left pedal") and the actual function is broken, and there is **no constraint** preventing the wrong action.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal Truth of HCI Design:**
> *"If a user makes a mistake using your system, it is almost always the designer's fault, not the user's fault."* — This is the central thesis of Donald Norman's work, and it is the philosophy the KTU examiner expects you to internalize.

**Another analogy:** Think of a light switch in a hotel room where you cannot tell whether the up position means "ON" or "OFF." You flip it, but the light does not change because the switch controls a different lamp. This is a **lack of feedback** + **poor mapping** mistake combined.

## 1.3 Classification Taxonomy — The Three Master Categories

All HCI design mistakes can be organized into three master categories:

| Master Category | Description | Norman Equivalent |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Conceptual Model Errors** | The designer fails to communicate how the system works to the user. | Gulf of Execution |
| **Mapping & Affordance Errors** | The physical controls do not naturally suggest their function. | Natural Mapping Violation |
| **Feedback & State Errors** | The system does not inform the user about the result of their action. | Gulf of Evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key HCI Metrics Used by KTU Examiners:**
> - **Discoverability:** Can the user figure out what actions are possible?
> - **Feedback:** Is there full and continuous information about the results of actions?
> - **Constraints:** Are there physical, logical, semantic, and cultural constraints that guide the user?
> - **Mapping:** Is the relationship between controls and their effects natural and obvious?
> - **Affordance:** Does the design suggest how it is to be used?

These **5 metrics** (in **bold** above) are the KTU-favorite definitions that examiners expect verbatim in 3-mark short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Frameworks

The study of design mistakes rests on three theoretical pillars. Each pillar provides a different lens through which to diagnose a defective design.

### 2.1.1 Norman's Seven Principles of Design (The "Why" Behind Mistakes)

Donald Norman articulated **seven fundamental design principles**. A design **mistake** occurs when one or more of these principles is violated.

**Principle 1: Discoverability**
- The user must be able to **discover** what actions are possible and how to perform them.
- **Mistake Example:** A mobile app with **invisible gesture controls** (e.g., a hidden swipe-to-delete feature that has no visual indicator).

**Principle 2: Feedback**
- After performing an action, the user must receive **full and continuous information** about the result.
- **Mistake Example:** Clicking a "Submit" button that shows no loading spinner, no confirmation, and no error — leaving the user wondering whether the action succeeded.

**Principle 3: Conceptual Model**
- The design must project a clear, consistent, and informative **conceptual model** of how the system works.
- **Mistake Example:** A file management system where files can be in multiple folders but the UI does not explain this — violating the user's mental model of a strict hierarchy.

**Principle 4: Affordances**
- The design must suggest the **affordances** — the actions that can be performed on an object.
- **Mistake Example:** A flat, glass-looking touchscreen button that does not visually afford "pressability."

**Principle 5: Signifiers**
- Visible cues (signifiers) must communicate **where the action should take place**.
- **Mistake Example:** A door handle that you have to push but looks like it should be pulled (the famous "Norman Door").

**Principle 6: Mappings**
- The relationship between **controls and their effects** must follow natural cultural or logical expectations.
- **Mistake Example:** A stove with four burner knobs arranged in a 2x2 grid but controlling burners in a completely different spatial layout.

**Principle 7: Constraints**
- Physical, logical, semantic, and cultural **constraints** should guide the user and prevent errors.
- **Mistake Example:** A form that allows the user to enter letters in a numeric-only phone number field with no validation constraint.

### 2.1.2 The Seven Stages of Action (Norman's Action Cycle)

Norman's **Action Cycle** is a 7-stage model used to pinpoint **where** in the cognitive process a design mistake causes a breakdown. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify which stage a particular error occurs at.

| Stage | User's Cognitive Task | Corresponding Gulf |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1. Forming the Goal** | What do I want to achieve? | — |
| **2. Forming the Intention** | What is my high-level plan? | **Gulf of Execution (Forming)** |
| **3. Specifying the Action** | What specific sequence will I execute? | **Gulf of Execution (Executing)** |
| **4. Executing the Action** | I physically perform the action. | — |
| **5. Perceiving the System State** | What happened in the physical world? | **Gulf of Evaluation (Perceiving)** |
| **6. Interpreting the State** | What does the result mean? | **Gulf of Evaluation (Interpreting)** |
| **7. Evaluating the Outcome** | Have I achieved my goal? | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A design mistake at **Stages 2–3** is an **Execution-side error** (the user does not know how to do something). A design mistake at **Stages 5–6** is an **Evaluation-side error** (the user did the action but does not know what happened). The KTU examiner expects you to **classify** a given design defect into one of these two categories for full marks.

### 2.1.3 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (The Practitioner's Checklist)

Jakob Nielsen's **10 Usability Heuristics** are the **de facto industry standard** for usability evaluation. A design mistake, in Nielsen's framework, is a **violation** of one or more heuristics. KTU 2024 Module 2 expects mastery of all 10.

| # | Heuristic Name | Definition of the Mistake Category |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **H1** | **Visibility of System Status** | The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. |
| **H2** | **Match Between System and Real World** | The system should speak the users' language, with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than system-oriented terms. |
| **H3** | **User Control and Freedom** | Users often perform actions by mistake. They need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted state without going through an extended dialogue. |
| **H4** | **Consistency and Standards** | Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Follow platform conventions. |
| **H5** | **Error Prevention** | Even better than good error messages is a careful design that prevents a problem from occurring in the first place. |
| **H6** | **Recognition Rather Than Recall** | Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible. The user should not have to remember information from one part of the dialogue to another. |
| **H7** | **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** | Accelerators (often unseen by the novice user) may often speed up the interaction for the expert user. |
| **H8** | **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** | Dialogues should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed. Every extra unit of information in a dialogue competes with the relevant information units. |
| **H9** | **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** | Error messages should be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution. |
| **H10** | **Help and Documentation** | Even though it is better if the system can be used without documentation, help and documentation may be necessary. Such information should be easy to search, focused on the user's task, list concrete steps, and not be too large. |

## 2.2 Deep Dive into the Top 10 Most Common Design Mistakes

Below is a structured classification of the **10 most frequently occurring** design mistakes in real-world computer systems, cross-referenced to Norman's principles and Nielsen's heuristics.

### Mistake 1: Invisibility (H1 Violation)
- **Description:** Critical functions or system states are hidden from the user.
- **Real-world Example:** A software installer that runs silently in the background with no progress window. The user does not know if the installation is frozen or progressing.
- **Why It Happens:** Designers assume the user "knows" what the system is doing.
- **Remediation:** Always show **system state** through progress bars, spinners, status icons, or notifications.

### Mistake 2: Poor Natural Mapping (Norman's Mapping Principle Violation)
- **Description:** The spatial or conceptual layout of controls does not correspond to their effects.
- **Real-world Example:** A TV remote where the **Volume Up** button is on the left and **Channel Up** is on the right — opposite to the on-screen OSD layout.
- **Why It Happens:** The designer arranges controls based on internal engineering logic (e.g., circuit board layout) rather than user mental models.
- **Remediation:** Apply **cultural and spatial conventions** (e.g., "up" = more, "down" = less).

### Mistake 3: No Feedback (H1 Violation)
- **Description:** The system performs an action but provides no sensory confirmation to the user.
- **Real-world Example:** A touchscreen that does not provide haptic or visual feedback when a button is tapped, making the user uncertain whether the touch was registered.
- **Remediation:** Provide **multimodal feedback** (visual + auditory + haptic).

### Mistake 4: Inconsistency (H4 Violation)
- **Description:** The same action is performed differently in different parts of the system, or the same icon means different things.
- **Real-world Example:** The "trash can" icon in one app permanently deletes a file, but in another app it moves the file to a recycle bin — with no explanation.
- **Remediation:** Maintain a **comprehensive style guide** and conduct **consistency audits** before release.

### Mistake 5: Information Overload (H8 Violation)
- **Description:** Too much information is presented on a single screen, overwhelming the user.
- **Real-world Example:** A medical dashboard that displays 47 different metrics simultaneously, making it impossible for a doctor to identify the critical ones.
- **Remediation:** Apply **progressive disclosure** and **information hierarchy** (primary, secondary, tertiary).

### Mistake 6: Poor Error Messages (H9 Violation)
- **Description:** Error messages are cryptic, technical, or blame the user.
- **Real-world Example:** Error message: `EXCEPTION 0xC0000005 at memory address 0x00000000.` This is a developer's message, not a user's message.
- **Remediation:** Use **plain language**, indicate the **problem**, and suggest a **constructive solution**. Example: *"We could not save your file because the disk is full. Please free up some space and try again."*

### Mistake 7: No Undo (H3 Violation)
- **Description:** The user cannot easily reverse an accidental or erroneous action.
- **Real-world Example:** A banking app that immediately transfers money with no confirmation dialog and no "undo" option.
- **Remediation:** Implement **multi-level undo** and **confirmation dialogs** for destructive actions.

### Mistake 8: Requiring the User to Remember (H6 Violation)
- **Description:** The interface forces the user to recall information from memory rather than recognizing it on screen.
- **Real-world Example:** A command-line interface that requires the user to type `tar -xzvf archive.tar.gz` from memory, with no menu showing available commands.
- **Remediation:** Use **menus, icons, and tooltips** to enable recognition.

### Mistake 9: Ignoring Accessibility
- **Description:** The design does not accommodate users with disabilities (visual, auditory, motor, cognitive).
- **Real-world Example:** A video player that has no captions, no audio descriptions, and cannot be operated by keyboard alone.
- **Remediation:** Follow **WCAG 2.1 (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines)** and the **POUR principles**: **Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust**.

### Mistake 10: Lack of Error Prevention (H5 Violation)
- **Description:** The design allows the user to make a mistake that could have been prevented.
- **Real-world Example:** A "Delete Account" button placed next to the "Save" button with no confirmation step. A user clicking the wrong button loses their entire account.
- **Remediation:** Apply **constraints** (disable destructive options temporarily), use **confirmation dialogs**, and implement **soft delete** patterns.

## 2.3 The Three Gulfs — Formal Definitions

The "Three Gulfs" model (sometimes called the "Gulf Model") is the most elegant theoretical framework for explaining design mistakes. Every design mistake can be mapped to one or more gulfs.

### 2.3.1 Gulf of Execution
- **Definition:** The gap between the **user's goal/intention** and the **means available** to achieve it through the system interface.
- **Design Mistake Implication:** If the gulf of execution is wide, the user does not know **how** to operate the system. The interface has failed to make the actions **discoverable** and **affordant**.
- **Metric to Reduce It:** Maximize the **signifiers** and **affordances** of controls.

### 2.3.2 Gulf of Evaluation
- **Definition:** The gap between the **physical system state** (what the system has done) and the **user's perception/understanding** of that state.
- **Design Mistake Implication:** If the gulf of evaluation is wide, the user cannot tell **what the system has done** or **what state it is in**. The interface has failed to provide adequate **feedback**.
- **Metric to Reduce It:** Provide **immediate, clear, and continuous** feedback.

### 2.3.3 Semantic Gap (The Third Gulf)
- **Definition:** The gap between the **user's mental model** (how the user thinks the system works) and the **system model** (how the system actually works).
- **Design Mistake Implication:** If the semantic gap is wide, the user forms **incorrect expectations** about the system, leading to **surprise, frustration, and errors**.
- **Metric to Reduce It:** Communicate the **conceptual model** clearly through documentation, onboarding, and intuitive defaults.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following "formulas" in HCI are not algebraic equations but **structured mnemonics and assessment frameworks** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in the exam hall.

| # | Framework | Symbolic Form | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Norman Action Cycle | $\text{Goal} \rightarrow \text{Intention} \rightarrow \text{Specification} \rightarrow \text{Execution} \rightarrow \text{Perception} \rightarrow \text{Interpretation} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation}$ | Diagnose **where** a design mistake occurs in the user's cognitive process. |
| 2 | Gulf of Execution Width | $W_{E} = f(\text{Affordance}, \text{Signifier}, \text{Constraint})$ | Quantify (qualitatively) how hard it is for the user to operate the system. |
| 3 | Gulf of Evaluation Width | $W_{Ev} = f(\text{Feedback Latency}, \text{Feedback Clarity})$ | Quantify (qualitatively) how hard it is for the user to assess system state. |
| 4 | Usability Score (Nielsen) | $\text{Usability} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} w_i \cdot H_i$ | Weighted sum of 10 heuristic compliance scores. |
| 5 | Error Prevention Ratio | $\text{EPR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{errors prevented}}}{N_{\text{errors possible}}}$ | Proportion of potential errors eliminated by design constraints. |
| 6 | Discoverability Index | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{discoverable features}}}{N_{\text{total features}}}$ | Proportion of features the user can find without instruction. |
| 7 | Fitts's Law (Pointing) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Predict time to acquire a target. **D** = distance, **W** = width. **Unit: milliseconds**. |
| 8 | Hick's Law (Choice) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$ | Predict decision time. **n** = number of choices. **Unit: milliseconds**. |
| 9 | WCAG POUR Principles | $P + O + U + R = \text{Accessible}$ | Accessibility is the sum of Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. |
| 10 | Severity Rating (Nielsen) | $S = \sum (\text{Frequency} \times \text{Impact} \times \text{Persistence})$ | Rate the severity of a usability problem. |

### 2.4.1 Fitts's Law — Detailed Explanation

Fitts's Law is a **predictive model** for the time required to rapidly move to a target area.

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

Where:
- $T$ = average time to complete the movement (in **seconds** or **milliseconds**)
- $a$ = start/stop time of the device (empirically derived constant)
- $b$ = inherent speed of the device (empirically derived constant)
- $D$ = distance from the starting point to the center of the target (in **pixels**, **cm**, or **inches**)
- $W$ = width of the target measured along the axis of motion (in same units as $D$)

**Design Mistake Implication:** If a button is **too small** (low $W$) or **too far away** (high $D$), the time $T$ to click it increases, leading to **user frustration and pointing errors**. Designers should therefore make frequently-used controls **large** and place them **close** to the user's typical cursor position.

### 2.4.2 Hick's Law — Detailed Explanation

Hick's Law predicts the time it takes for a person to make a decision as a function of the number of possible choices.

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$$

Where:
- $T$ = decision time (in **seconds** or **milliseconds**)
- $a$ = non-decision time (perception + motor execution constant)
- $b$ = empirically derived constant
- $n$ = number of equally probable choices

**Design Mistake Implication:** If a menu presents **too many options** at the same hierarchical level, the decision time $T$ increases logarithmically. Designers should therefore **group** related options into sub-menus (reducing $n$ at any one level), a technique known as **progressive disclosure**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Severity Rating Formula

The Nielsen **Severity Rating** for a usability problem is used to **prioritize** which design mistakes to fix first. It is a **heuristic composite metric** with three components. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Three Component Factors

Let us assign formal symbols to each factor as understood in HCI literature:

$$\text{Frequency} = F \quad \text{(How often the problem occurs)}$$

$$\text{Impact} = I \quad \text{(How severely the problem affects the user)}$$

$$\text{Persistence} = P \quad \text{(How long the problem persists after the first occurrence)}$$

### Step 2: Define the Rating Scales

Each factor is rated on a scale from **0 to 4**, where:
- **0** = Not a problem at all
- **1** = Cosmetic problem only
- **2** = Minor usability problem
- **3** = Major usability problem
- **4** = Usability catastrophe

### Step 3: Combine the Factors

The composite severity score is the **multiplicative product** of the three factors, plus an optional **"Global Problem"** multiplier of **1.5** for issues that affect every user in every session:

$$S = F \times I \times P$$

For global problems:

$$S_{global} = 1.5 \times F \times I \times P$$

### Step 4: Interpretation of the Resulting Score

| Score Range | Severity Level | Recommended Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $0 \leq S \leq 3$ | Cosmetic | Fix only if time permits |
| $4 \leq S \leq 7$ | Minor | Low-priority fix |
| $8 \leq S \leq 14$ | Major | High-priority fix |
| $15 \leq S \leq 20$ | Catastrophe | Must fix before release |

### Step 3.1: Worked Example

**Scenario:** A user clicks the "Send" button on an email client, and the system provides **no feedback** for 12 seconds, then either sends the email silently or fails silently with no error message. The problem occurs **every time** ($F = 4$), it is **catastrophic** because the user does not know if their critical email was sent ($I = 4$), and the problem **persists** because there is no error log ($P = 4$).

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$S = F \times I \times P = 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64$$

**Normalize against the scale ceiling (which is $4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64$):**

$$\text{Normalized Severity} = \dfrac{64}{64} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

This is the **maximum possible severity** — a **usability catastrophe** that must be fixed.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fitts's Law (Conceptual Derivation)

Fitts's Law emerged from **Shannon's Theorem 17** on the rate of information transmission. The derivation proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Define the Index of Difficulty

The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is defined as the logarithmic ratio of distance to width:

$$\text{ID} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{(measured in bits)}$$

The **"+1"** term prevents the logarithm from going to negative infinity when the target is very wide or very close. The unit **bits** comes from information theory: one "bit" of difficulty corresponds to halving the target width or doubling the distance.

### Step 2: Define the Movement Time

Fitts observed empirically that movement time $T$ is **linearly proportional** to the Index of Difficulty:

$$T = a + b \cdot \text{ID}$$

### Step 3: Substitute

Substituting the definition of ID into the linear model:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

This is the canonical Fitts's Law equation. The constants $a$ and $b$ are determined empirically through controlled pointing experiments (e.g., $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 150$ ms/bit for a standard mouse).

### Step 4: Apply to a Design Mistake Diagnosis

**Scenario:** A "Submit" button is placed **300 pixels** away from the user's current cursor position, and it is only **40 pixels wide**. What is the movement time?

**Given:**
$$D = 300 \text{ pixels}, \quad W = 40 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$\text{ID} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{300}{40} + 1\right) = \log_2(7.5 + 1) = \log_2(8.5)$$

$$\log_2(8.5) = \dfrac{\ln(8.5)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.140}{0.693} \approx 3.088 \text{ bits}$$

Using typical mouse constants $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit:

$$T = 50 + 150 \times 3.088 = 50 + 463.2 = 513.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Interpretation:** The user will take approximately **513 milliseconds** to move to and click this button. If the button were doubled in width to 80 pixels, the new ID would be:

$$\text{ID}_{new} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{300}{80} + 1\right) = \log_2(3.75 + 1) = \log_2(4.75) \approx 2.248 \text{ bits}$$

$$T_{new} = 50 + 150 \times 2.248 = 50 + 337.2 = 387.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Result:** Doubling the button width saves approximately **126 ms** per click — a **24% improvement** in interaction time. This quantifies the cost of the design mistake (button too small) and the benefit of the remediation (enlarge the button).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Mistakes to Frameworks

The following matrix is the **KTU examiner's favorite format** for testing cross-referencing ability. It maps each of the **10 most common design mistakes** to **all three theoretical frameworks** (Norman's Principles, Norman's Action Cycle, Nielsen's Heuristics).

| # | Design Mistake | Norman Principle Violated | Action Cycle Stage of Breakdown | Nielsen Heuristic Violated |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Invisibility | Discoverability | Stage 2 (Intention) | H1 |
| 2 | Poor Natural Mapping | Mapping | Stage 3 (Specification) | H2 |
| 3 | No Feedback | Feedback | Stage 5 (Perception) | H1 |
| 4 | Inconsistency | Conceptual Model | Stage 3 (Specification) | H4 |
| 5 | Information Overload | Conceptual Model | Stage 6 (Interpretation) | H8 |
| 6 | Poor Error Messages | Feedback | Stage 6 (Interpretation) | H9 |
| 7 | No Undo | Constraints | Stage 7 (Evaluation) | H3 |
| 8 | Recall vs Recognition | Conceptual Model | Stage 3 (Specification) | H6 |
| 9 | Ignoring Accessibility | Affordances | Stage 4 (Execution) | H10 (indirectly) |
| 10 | No Error Prevention | Constraints | Stage 3 (Specification) | H5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Which heuristic is violated by this design?", always cite the **Nielsen heuristic number** (e.g., **H5 — Error Prevention**) and **quote the exact heuristic definition** from your study material. Do not paraphrase — the examiner has a marking key that looks for verbatim definitions.

## 3.4 The Cognitive Walkthrough Method (Analytical Procedure)

A **cognitive walkthrough** is a formal, step-by-step procedure for **detecting design mistakes** by simulating the user's cognitive process. The KTU examiner often asks students to describe this method.

### Step 1: Identify the User's Goal
Define what the user is trying to accomplish (e.g., "Send an email with an attachment").

### Step 2: Define the Correct Action Sequence
List the exact sequence of actions the user must perform to achieve the goal (e.g., "Click Compose → Click Attach → Select file → Click Open → Click Send").

### Step 3: For Each Action, Ask Four Questions

For every step in the sequence, the evaluator asks:

**Q1:** Will the user try to achieve the right effect?
(Does the user know what to do at this step?)

**Q2:** Will the user notice that the correct action is available?
(Are the controls visible and discoverable?)

**Q3:** Will the user associate the correct action with the effect they are trying to achieve?
(Is the mapping natural and obvious?)

**Q4:** Will the user get feedback that the action was successful?
(Does the system confirm the action's completion?)

### Step 4: Score the Walkthrough

If the answer to **any** of the four questions is **"No"** for any step, a design mistake has been identified. The severity of the mistake is rated using the Nielsen severity formula from Section 3.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The four questions of the cognitive walkthrough are one of the highest-frequency 3-mark questions in the KTU ESE question bank for this module. Memorize them as **Q1–Q4** in the order presented above.

## 4.1 Norman's Action Cycle with Gulf Identification

This diagram maps the **seven stages of action** and identifies **where the gulfs occur**. It is the foundational mental model for diagnosing design mistakes.

```mermaid
    interpNode["Stage 6: Interpreting State"]
    evalNode["Stage 7: Evaluating the Outcome"]

    goalNode --> intentNode
    intentNode -- "Gulf of Execution" --> specNode
    specNode -- "Gulf of Execution" --> execNode
    execNode -- "Physical Action" --> percepNode
    percepNode -- "Gulf of Evaluation" --> interpNode
    interpNode -- "Gulf of Evaluation" --> evalNode
    evalNode -.->|"If Goal Not Met, Loop Back"| goalNode

```

**Color Key:**
- **Blue** = Cognitive/Goal-formation stage (pre-action)

- **Purple** = Outcome evaluation (loop-back trigger)

This flowchart provides a **decision tree** for classifying an observed design defect into one of the three master categories of mistakes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    cat1["CATEGORY 1: EXECUTION-SIDE MISTAKE\nGulf of Execution\nAffordance, Signifier, Mapping Violation"]
    cat2["CATEGORY 2: EVALUATION-SIDE MISTAKE\nGulf of Evaluation\nFeedback Violation"]

    startNode --> q1
    q1 -- "NO" --> cat1
    q1 -- "YES" --> q2
    q2 -- "NO" --> cat2
    q2 -- "YES" --> q3

    q3 -- "NO" --> unresolved["Unclassified: Requires Deeper Heuristic Audit"]

```

## 4.4 Cross-Reference Matrix Diagram

This diagram shows the **relationships** between the three theoretical frameworks. KTU examiners often expect students to draw or describe this web of relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    norman["NORMAN FRAMEWORK\nPrinciples + Action Cycle + Gulfs"]
    nielsen["NIELSEN FRAMEWORK\n10 Usability Heuristics"]
    walk["COGNITIVE WALKTHROUGH\n4-Question Method"]
    norman -- "Maps to" --> nielsen
    nielsen -- "Diagnosed by" --> walk
    walk -- "Traces back to" --> norman

    norman -.->|"Why mistakes occur"| context["CONTEXT: User Mental Model"]

    walk -.->|"Where in the action sequence"| context

    style norman fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nielsen fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style walk fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style context fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc
```

**Interpretation:** The three frameworks are **complementary**, not redundant. Norman provides the **theory** (why mistakes happen), Nielsen provides the **checklist** (what to look for), and the cognitive walkthrough provides the **procedure** (how to systematically detect mistakes).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the following terms with a one-line example each
**(a) Affordance** **(b) Signifier** **(c) Mapping**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Affordance:** An affordance is a **relationship between an object and a person** that suggests what actions can be performed on it. *Example:* A door handle affords "pulling" because of its shape.

**(b) Signifier:** A signifier is **any perceivable cue** (visual, auditory, tactile) that communicates where the action should take place. *Example:* The "Click here" text on a button is a signifier indicating that the button is clickable.

**(c) Mapping:** Mapping is the **relationship between a control and its resulting effect** in the world. *Example:* The spatial arrangement of stove burner knobs should map naturally to the spatial arrangement of the burners on the cooktop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **affordance** and **signifier**. Remember: **Affordance = what an object allows you to do** (perceived property), while **Signifier = the visual/auditory cue that communicates the affordance** (deliberate communication). An affordance can exist without a signifier (a hidden handle still affords pulling), but a signifier exists to make an affordance **discoverable**.

---

### Question 2: State and briefly explain Norman's Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### Model Answer:

**Gulf of Execution:** The Gulf of Execution is the **gap between the user's intentions/goals and the means available in the system to accomplish those goals**. A wide gulf of execution means the user does not know how to operate the system. It is reduced by improving **affordances, signifiers, and natural mappings**.

**Gulf of Evaluation:** The Gulf of Evaluation is the **gap between the physical state of the system and the user's perception/understanding of that state**. A wide gulf of evaluation means the user does not understand what the system has done or what state it is in. It is reduced by providing **immediate, clear, and continuous feedback**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When the question asks for the **"difference"** between the two gulfs, students must explicitly state the **direction** of the gap: Execution is **goal → system** (user trying to do something), while Evaluation is **system → goal** (user trying to understand what the system did). Reversing the direction is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **Donald Norman's Seven Principles of Design** in detail. For each principle, provide **one example of a design mistake** that violates that principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Jakob Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics**. For any **three** heuristics, give a real-world example of a design mistake and propose a concrete remediation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Seven Principles of Design, as articulated by Donald Norman, are the foundational criteria for evaluating the quality of an interactive design. A design mistake occurs when one or more of these principles is violated.

**Principle 1: Discoverability**
It should be possible to discover what actions are possible and how to perform them. *Design Mistake Example:* A mobile app hides the "Delete" functionality behind a long-press gesture with no visual indicator. *Remediation:* Show a visible "trash" icon next to each item that the user can tap to delete.

**Principle 2: Feedback**
There must be full and continuous information about the results of actions. *Design Mistake Example:* Clicking "Submit" on a web form shows no spinner, no progress bar, and no confirmation. *Remediation:* Display a loading spinner during submission and a success toast notification upon completion.

**Principle 3: Conceptual Model**
The design must project a clear and consistent conceptual model of how the system works. *Design Mistake Example:* A cloud storage service that has no clear model of "folders" vs. "tags," leaving users confused about file organization. *Remediation:* Provide a clear onboarding tour that explains the storage model.

**Principle 4: Affordances**
The design must suggest the affordances — the actions that can be performed. *Design Mistake Example:* A flat, glass-like button on a touchscreen that does not visually afford "pressability." *Remediation:* Add subtle shadows, gradients, or raised edges to suggest the button can be pressed.

**Principle 5: Signifiers**
Visible cues must communicate where the action should take place. *Design Mistake Example:* A door with a handle that you have to push (a "Norman Door") but the handle suggests pulling. *Remediation:* Replace the handle with a flat push plate that correctly signifiers "push."

**Principle 6: Mappings**
The relationship between controls and their effects must be natural. *Design Mistake Example:* Car seat heating controls that are not located near the seats they control. *Remediation:* Place each control on or near the seat it controls, following a spatial mapping convention.

**Principle 7: Constraints**
Physical, logical, semantic, and cultural constraints must guide the user. *Design Mistake Example:* A date-of-birth field that accepts "February 30" with no validation. *Remediation:* Implement logical constraints to reject invalid dates.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Jakob Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics, published in 1994, are the industry-standard checklist for usability evaluation. Each heuristic represents a category of design mistakes to avoid.

**H1: Visibility of System Status** — The system should always keep users informed about what is going on. *Mistake Example:* A file upload that shows no progress bar. *Remediation:* Display a real-time progress indicator with percentage completion.

**H2: Match Between System and the Real World** — The system should speak the users' language. *Mistake Example:* An error message that says "Error 404: Resource not found at URI /api/v2/users." *Remediation:* Rewrite as "We could not find the user you were looking for. Please check the name and try again."

**H3: User Control and Freedom** — Users need a clearly marked "emergency exit." *Mistake Example:* A multi-step checkout process that has no "Back" button. *Remediation:* Add a "Back" button at every step and support the browser's back button.

**H4: Consistency and Standards** — Follow platform conventions. *Mistake Example:* An Android app that uses iOS-style navigation patterns. *Remediation:* Follow Material Design guidelines for Android and Human Interface Guidelines for iOS.

**H5: Error Prevention** — Good design prevents problems before they occur. *Mistake Example:* A "Delete" button next to "Save" with no confirmation. *Remediation:* Move destructive actions to a separate "Danger Zone" section and require confirmation.

**H6: Recognition Rather than Recall** — Minimize memory load. *Mistake Example:* A command-line tool with 50 commands and no menu. *Remediation:* Provide a menu-driven interface and command history.

**H7: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** — Provide accelerators for experts. *Mistake Example:* A photo editor that only supports mouse operations. *Remediation:* Add keyboard shortcuts for common actions.

**H8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** — Avoid irrelevant information. *Mistake Example:* A medical dashboard showing 47 metrics at once. *Remediation:* Show 5 critical metrics by default and let the user drill down for more.

**H9: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** — Error messages should be in plain language. *Mistake Example:* "Exception 0xC0000005." *Remediation:* "Your password must be at least 8 characters. Please try again."

**H10: Help and Documentation** — Provide searchable help. *Mistake Example:* A 500-page PDF manual with no search. *Remediation:* Provide a searchable in-app help center with task-based articles.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is a **Cognitive Walkthrough**? Explain the **four questions** asked at each step of the walkthrough. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **Fitts's Law** and **Hick's Law**, explain how each law predicts user performance and how violating the design principles derived from these laws leads to design mistakes. Use **numerical examples** to support your answer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Cognitive Walkthrough** is a **formal, expert-based usability evaluation method** in which one or more evaluators simulate the cognitive process of a user attempting to accomplish a specific task on the interface. The method was developed by **Wharton, Rieman, Lewis, and Polson** in 1994. It is specifically designed to **detect learnability problems** in walk-up-and-use systems.

The procedure consists of the following steps:

**Step 1:** Define the **user persona** (e.g., a first-time user, an expert user).
**Step 2:** Define the **task** to be accomplished (e.g., "Send an email with an attachment").
**Step 3:** Define the **correct action sequence** the user must perform.
**Step 4:** For each action in the sequence, ask the four critical questions:

**Q1: Will the user try to achieve the right effect?**
This checks whether the user has the **knowledge** to know what to do at this step. If the user has never used an email client before, they may not know they need to click "Compose" first. A "No" answer indicates a **discoverability mistake** (Nielsen H1).

**Q2: Will the user notice that the correct action is available?**
This checks whether the **correct control is visible and findable** in the interface. If the "Attach" button is hidden in a sub-menu, the user may not notice it. A "No" answer indicates a **visibility mistake** (Nielsen H1).

**Q3: Will the user associate the correct action with the effect they are trying to achieve?**
This checks whether the **mapping is natural**. If the "Attach" button is labeled "Insert Object" (a technical term), the user may not associate it with attaching a file. A "No" answer indicates a **language/mapping mistake** (Nielsen H2).

**Q4: Will the user get feedback that the action was successful?**
This checks whether the system **confirms the action**. If the user clicks "Attach" and no file appears in the attachment list, they will not know whether the action succeeded. A "No" answer indicates a **feedback mistake** (Nielsen H1, H9).

**Output:** If any question receives a "No" answer, a **design mistake** is identified. The walkthrough concludes with a **severity rating** for each identified problem.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Fitts's Law** predicts the time required to **rapidly move a pointer (mouse, finger) to a target area**. The law is:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

where $T$ is the movement time, $D$ is the distance to the target, $W$ is the width of the target, and $a$ and $b$ are device-dependent empirical constants.

**Design Mistake Implication:** If a button is too small ($W$ small) or too far away ($D$ large), the movement time $T$ increases. This leads to user frustration, pointing errors, and reduced efficiency. **Design Rule:** Place frequently-used controls **close** to the user's current position and make them **large** enough to be easily acquired.

**Numerical Example:** A "Submit" button is located **500 pixels** away from the cursor, and it is **50 pixels wide**. Using typical mouse constants $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit:

$$\text{ID} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{500}{50} + 1\right) = \log_2(11) \approx 3.459 \text{ bits}$$

$$T = 50 + 150 \times 3.459 = 50 + 518.9 = 568.9 \text{ ms}$$

If the button width is **doubled** to 100 pixels:

$$\text{ID}_{new} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{500}{100} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}$$

$$T_{new} = 50 + 150 \times 2.585 = 50 + 387.7 = 437.7 \text{ ms}$$

**Result:** Doubling the button width saves **131 ms** per click — a **23% improvement**.

---

**Hick's Law** predicts the time it takes to **make a decision** as a function of the number of available choices:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$$

where $T$ is the decision time, $n$ is the number of equally probable choices, and $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants.

**Design Mistake Implication:** If a menu presents too many options ($n$ large) at the same hierarchical level, the decision time $T$ increases logarithmically. This is the classic **information overload** mistake. **Design Rule:** Group related options into sub-menus and use **progressive disclosure** to reduce $n$ at each level.

**Numerical Example:** A toolbar presents **16 commands** at the same level. Using typical constants $a = 100$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit:

$$T = 100 + 150 \times \log_2(16) = 100 + 150 \times 4 = 700 \text{ ms}$$

If the 16 commands are reorganized into **4 groups of 4** (requiring the user to first select a group, then a command):

$$T_{group} = 100 + 150 \times \log_2(4) = 100 + 150 \times 2 = 400 \text{ ms (first selection)}$$

$$T_{command} = 100 + 150 \times \log_2(4) = 400 \text{ ms (second selection)}$$

$$T_{total} = 400 + 400 = 800 \text{ ms}$$

**Result:** Grouping does **not** reduce Hick's Law time when the user must make **two sequential decisions**, but it **does** reduce the **cognitive load** at each decision point, which is the more important factor for learnability and error reduction. For experts, a single-level menu of 16 may be faster, but for novices, the grouped menu reduces **errors** and **anxiety**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. When solving Fitts's Law numerical problems, always include the **"+1"** in the logarithm. Omitting it is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. When solving Hick's Law, remember that $n$ must be the **number of equally probable choices**. If probabilities are unequal, use the **weighted entropy** formula instead: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$. The KTU examiner may test this advanced version.
> 3. For **internal choice** questions, the examiner will mark **only the first attempted answer** unless the student **clearly crosses out** the unwanted one. Do not waste time answering both.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

### Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim)
- ☐ **Affordance:** Relationship between object and person that suggests possible actions.
- ☐ **Signifier:** Perceivable cue that communicates where the action should take place.
- ☐ **Mapping:** Relationship between control and its effect.
- ☐ **Constraint:** Physical, logical, semantic, or cultural limitation that guides the user.
- ☐ **Feedback:** Full and continuous information about the result of an action.
- ☐ **Conceptual Model:** A clear, consistent, and informative explanation of how the system works.
- ☐ **Discoverability:** The user can figure out what actions are possible and how to perform them.

### Norman's Gulfs
- ☐ **Gulf of Execution:** Goal → System (user does not know HOW to do it). Fix with affordances, signifiers, constraints.
- ☐ **Gulf of Evaluation:** System → Goal (user does not know WHAT happened). Fix with feedback.
- ☐ **Semantic Gap:** User's mental model ≠ System's actual model. Fix with onboarding, documentation.

### Norman's Seven Action Cycle Stages (in order)
- ☐ 1. Forming the Goal → 2. Forming the Intention → 3. Specifying the Action → 4. Executing the Action → 5. Perceiving System State → 6. Interpreting State → 7. Evaluating the Outcome.

### Nielsen's 10 Heuristics (Mnemonic: "Very Many Users Complain About Real Crappy Heuristic Evaluation Forms")
- ☐ H1: **V**isibility of System Status
- ☐ H2: **M**atch Between System and Real World
- ☐ H3: **U**ser Control and Freedom
- ☐ H4: **C**onsistency and Standards
- ☐ H5: **E**rror Prevention
- ☐ H6: **R**ecognition Rather Than Recall
- ☐ H7: **C**rappy → **F**lexibility and Efficiency of Use
- ☐ H8: **H**euristic → **A**esthetic and Minimalist Design
- ☐ H9: **E**valuation → **H**elp Users Recognize, Diagnose, Recover
- ☐ H10: **F**orms → **H**elp and Documentation

### Quantitative Laws
- ☐ **Fitts's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ — Predicts pointing time. **Rule:** Make targets **large** and **close**.
- ☐ **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$ — Predicts decision time. **Rule:** Reduce $n$ through grouping and progressive disclosure.

### Severity Rating
- ☐ $S = F \times I \times P$ (Frequency × Impact × Persistence, each rated 0–4).
- ☐ Scale: **0–3 Cosmetic**, **4–7 Minor**, **8–14 Major**, **15+ Catastrophe**.

### Cognitive Walkthrough Four Questions (in order)
- ☐ Q1: Will the user try the right effect?
- ☐ Q2: Will the user notice the correct action is available?
- ☐ Q3: Will the user associate the action with the effect?
- ☐ Q4: Will the user get feedback that the action succeeded?

### KTU Frequently Tested Mistakes (Top 5)
- ☐ **No feedback** (H1)
- ☐ **Inconsistency** (H4)
- ☐ **No undo** (H3)
- ☐ **Cryptic error messages** (H9)
- ☐ **Information overload** (H8)

### Final KTU Exam Strategy
- ☐ For **3-mark** questions: Quote definitions **verbatim** and provide **one concrete example**.
- ☐ For **14-mark** questions: Use **headings, sub-points, and diagrams** (Mermaid-friendly in descriptive form).
- ☐ For **internal choice**: Attempt **only one** option; do not waste time on both.
- ☐ Always **cross-reference** mistakes to **both** Norman and Nielsen frameworks for full marks.
- ☐ For **numerical problems**: Show **all steps** of calculation, including the **"+1"** in Fitts's Law.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interaction with Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Interaction with Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** is a multidisciplinary field of study focused on the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, and the study of the major phenomena surrounding them. It bridges the gap between the *user (the human)* and the *machine (the computer system)* by integrating principles from **computer science, cognitive psychology, design, ergonomics, and human factors engineering**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a small child how to use a television remote control. The child knows what they *want* (to watch their favorite cartoon), but the remote is the gateway between their **intent** and the **result**. If the buttons are labeled clearly, the layout is intuitive, and the response is instant, the child uses it happily. If the labels are cryptic, the buttons are placed in a confusing order, or the response is delayed, the child gets frustrated and gives up.

In this analogy:
- The **child** = the **human (user)**.
- The **remote control** = the **interface**.
- The **television** = the **computer system**.
- The **process of learning, pressing, watching** = the **interaction**.

HCI, in essence, is the engineering discipline that ensures the "remote control" of every digital product is as effortless, natural, and efficient as possible for every kind of user.

### Key Terminology of HCI

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **User** | The individual (or group) who interacts with the system to achieve a specific goal. |
| **Goal** | The desired outcome the user wants to accomplish (e.g., send an email, plot a graph). |
| **Task** | The sequence of operations required to achieve a goal. |
| **Interface** | The boundary across which interaction takes place (hardware + software). |
| **Usability** | A measure of the ease with which a user can perform a task. |
| **User Experience (UX)** | The overall feeling, perception, and satisfaction of a user when interacting with a system. |
| **Affordance** | A property of an object that suggests how it can be used (e.g., a button *affords* pressing). |
| **Feedback** | Information sent back to the user about the result of an action. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus explicitly stresses that HCI is **NOT** just about "making things look pretty." It is a rigorous engineering discipline that combines measurable human performance metrics with software design.

### The Three Pillars of HCI (Foundational Domains)

HCI sits at the intersection of three distinct academic bodies of knowledge:

1. **The Human (Psychology / Ergonomics):** Understanding human sensory channels (vision, hearing, touch), cognitive limits (short-term memory holds **7 ± 2 items** as per Miller's Law), and motor skills (reaction time, dexterity).
2. **The Computer (Computer Science & Engineering):** Input/output hardware, display technologies, programming paradigms, and system architecture.
3. **The Interaction (Design & Cognitive Science):** Dialog design, error handling, information architecture, and usability evaluation methodologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Usability Metrics used in KTU evaluations:**
> - **Learnability:** How easy is it for new users to accomplish basic tasks?
> - **Efficiency:** How quickly can experienced users perform tasks?
> - **Memorability:** Can users re-establish proficiency after a period of non-use?
> - **Errors:** How many errors do users make, how severe are they, and can they recover easily?
> - **Satisfaction:** How pleasant is the design to use?
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Importance of Human-Computer Interaction

Modern computing is no longer limited to expert programmers in air-conditioned server rooms. Today, computing is embedded in **smartphones, ATMs, medical devices, automobiles, and industrial control panels**. Poor HCI design leads to:

- **Catastrophic failures** (e.g., the Therac-25 radiation overdose incidents, attributed to poor interface design).
- **Financial loss** (e.g., abandoned e-commerce carts due to confusing checkout flows).
- **Productivity loss** (e.g., workers requiring extensive training to use enterprise software).
- **User frustration, stress, and physical injury** (e.g., Repetitive Strain Injury from poorly designed keyboards).

### 2.2 The Historical Evolution of HCI (Framework for KTU Answers)

- **1950s–1960s:** Batch processing; users were forced to adapt to the machine. No real "interaction."
- **1970s:** Introduction of the **Xerox PARC Graphical User Interface (GUI)** — windows, icons, menus, pointers (**WIMP**) became the gold standard.
- **1980s:** Personal computers (Apple Macintosh, IBM PC) democratized computing; usability emerged as a concern.
- **1990s:** The World Wide Web introduced **hypertext navigation**, raising new usability challenges.
- **2000s:** Mobile computing (iPhone, 2007) introduced **multi-touch, gestures, and small-screen interaction**.
- **2010s–Present:** **Natural User Interfaces (NUI)** — voice assistants (Siri, Alexa), VR/AR headsets, brain-computer interfaces, and **Conversational AI**.

### 2.3 The Interaction Framework: Don Norman's Model

Don Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** is a foundational HCI model (heavily tested in KTU). It describes the cognitive bridge between what a user *wants* to do and what the system *actually does*.

| Stage | Description | Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Goal Formation | The user forms a high-level goal ("I want to print this document"). | Cognition |
| 2. Plan Formation | The user formulates an intention to act. | Cognition |
| 3. Specification | The user specifies an action sequence ("Click File → Print"). | Cognition |
| 4. Execution | The user physically performs the action. | Action |
| 5. Perception | The user perceives the system's state (e.g., printer dialog opens). | Perception |
| 6. Interpretation | The user interprets the perception. | Perception |
| 7. Evaluation | The user evaluates the outcome against the original goal. | Evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gulf of Execution:** The gap between the user's intention and the physical actions allowed by the system. **Good design narrows this gulf.**
> **Gulf of Evaluation:** The gap between the system's physical state and the user's understanding of that state. **Good feedback narrows this gulf.**

### 2.4 Human-Centered Design (HCD) Lifecycle — ISO 9241-210

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates familiarity with the iterative HCD cycle:

1. **Understand and Specify the Context of Use** (Who are the users? What is the environment?).
2. **Specify the User Requirements** (Functional + non-functional requirements).
3. **Produce Design Solutions** (Wireframes, prototypes, mockups).
4. **Evaluate the Designs** (Usability testing with real users).
5. **Iterate and Refine** until usability goals are met.

### 2.5 Interaction Styles (The Vocabulary of HCI)

| Interaction Style | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Command Line Interface (CLI)** | User types textual commands. | DOS, Linux terminal |
| **Menu-Driven** | Hierarchical list of options. | Older ATM interfaces |
| **Form-Based** | User fills structured fields. | Online registration forms |
| **Direct Manipulation** | Visual representation of objects; actions are reversible. | Desktop drag-and-drop, Photoshop |
| **WIMP (GUI)** | Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointers. | Windows OS, macOS |
| **Natural Language** | Spoken or typed conversational input. | ChatGPT, Google Search |
| **Touch & Gesture** | Multi-touch, swipe, pinch, tap. | Smartphones, tablets |
| **Haptic / 3D** | Force feedback, motion sensing. | VR controllers, surgical robots |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Quantitative HCI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These formulas are highly tested in numerical HCI problems. Memorize the variables, units, and applicability.**

| Formula | Name | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Fitts' Law** | $MT$ = Movement Time, $D$ = Distance to target, $W$ = Width of target, $a,b$ = empirically fitted constants. | $MT$ in seconds, $D$ and $W$ in pixels/cm. | Predicts time to move a pointer to a target. Used to design button sizes and menu layouts. |
| $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID$ = Index of Difficulty, $D$ = Distance, $W$ = Width. | **bits** | Measures the perceptual-motor difficulty of a task. |
| $TP = \dfrac{ID}{MT}$ | **Throughput (TP)** | $TP$ = Throughput, $ID$ = Index of Difficulty, $MT$ = Movement Time. | **bits/second** | Measures human performance; higher TP means a more efficient interface. |
| $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)$ | **Hick's Law** | $RT$ = Reaction Time, $n$ = number of equally probable choices. | $RT$ in seconds, $n$ dimensionless. | Predicts decision time. More menu options $\rightarrow$ slower decisions. |
| $T_{steer} = a + b \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ | **Steering Law (Accot-Zhai)** | $T_{steer}$ = Steering time, $D$ = Path length, $W$ = Width of tunnel. | seconds | Predicts time to navigate through a constrained path (e.g., scrollbars, sliders). |
| $S = \dfrac{F}{N - 1}$ | **Speed-Accuracy Trade-off** | $S$ = Speed, $F$ = Fraction of correct responses, $N$ = total responses. | dimensionless | Used in Fitts' Law tapping tasks to validate experimental data. |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of HCI

- **Aerospace Cockpits:** Pilots' lives depend on legible, glare-resistant displays and grouped, color-coded controls (HCD applied).
- **Medical Devices:** Anesthesia machines and infusion pumps use **Fitts' Law**-optimized button sizes so a stressed nurse can hit the correct target in a single rapid movement.
- **Automotive HMI:** Touchscreen infotainment systems are designed using **Hick's Law** — driver distraction is minimized by limiting menu depth to a small number of choices.
- **Industrial Control Rooms:** Nuclear power plant operators use **Norman's Gulf of Evaluation** principles — every system state change must provide unambiguous, immediate feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fitts' Law (The Shannon-Hartley Theorem Analogy)

Fitts' Law is the **single most important quantitative law in HCI**, modeled directly after Claude Shannon's information-theoretic channel capacity theorem. The derivation proceeds by analogy with a noisy communication channel.

**Step 1: Start with the Shannon-Hartley Theorem for Channel Capacity.**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

This equation defines the maximum reliable information transmission rate $C$ (in bits/second) over a channel of bandwidth $B$ with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

**Step 2: Define an Analogy Between Communication and Motor Control.**

Paul Fitts observed that a human performing a rapid aimed movement (like clicking a small target) is analogous to a communication system transmitting a signal over a noisy channel. He mapped the variables:

- **Distance to target ($D$)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Signal amplitude.**
- **Width of target ($W$)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Noise (uncertainty).**
- **Movement time ($MT$)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Channel capacity / inverse of bandwidth.**
- **Index of Difficulty ($ID$)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Information transmitted (in bits).**

**Step 3: Compute the Index of Difficulty ($ID$).**

Replacing the signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ with $D/W$, and taking the channel capacity $C$ to represent the difficulty:

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

The "$+1$" term prevents $ID$ from becoming negative when $D$ is very small.

**Step 4: Express Movement Time as a Linear Function of $ID$.**

Assuming a linear relationship between information and time, Fitts proposed:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $ID$ to Recover the Standard Fitts' Law Equation.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

A more common empirical form uses a constant of 2 (from MacKenzie's re-examination):

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to Compute Throughput (Human Performance Metric).**

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT}
$$

A higher $TP$ indicates a more efficient user-interface design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Fitts' Law tells us that **bigger targets** (larger $W$) and **closer targets** (smaller $D$) are faster and easier to hit. This is the theoretical foundation behind every "large, central, primary action button" in modern mobile UI design.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Applying Fitts' Law

> **Problem (KTU Style):**
> A user is performing a one-dimensional tapping task. The distance to the target $D = 256$ pixels, and the target width $W = 32$ pixels. The empirically fitted constants for a standard mouse are $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit. Calculate the **Movement Time ($MT$)**, **Index of Difficulty ($ID$)**, and **Throughput ($TP$)**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Index of Difficulty.**

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{256}{32} + 1\right) = \log_{2}(8 + 1) = \log_{2}(9)
$$

Evaluating $\log_{2}(9)$ using the change-of-base formula $\log_{2}(x) = \frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)}$:

$$
ID = \frac{\ln(9)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.17 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Movement Time.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 256}{32}\right)
$$

$$
= 50 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{512}{32}\right) = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(16) = 50 + 150 \cdot 4 = 650 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Throughput.**

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{3.17}{0.650} \approx 4.88 \text{ bits/second}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Conclusion.**

A throughput of $\approx 4.88$ bits/second is consistent with typical mouse-based pointing performance (literature values range from $3$ to $6$ bits/sec). The interface is performing within acceptable usability parameters.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Hick's Law for Menu Design

> **Problem (KTU Style):**
> A mobile app's main menu has **8** equally probable icons. Empirical constants for finger-tap selection are $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms. Calculate the reaction time. If the designer reduces the menu to **4** equally probable icons, what is the percentage reduction in reaction time?

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate RT for $n = 8$.**

$$
RT_{8} = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n) = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_{2}(8) = 100 + 150 \cdot 3 = 550 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate RT for $n = 4$.**

$$
RT_{4} = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(4) = 100 + 150 \cdot 2 = 400 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Percentage Reduction.**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{RT_{8} - RT_{4}}{RT_{8}} \cdot 100 = \frac{550 - 400}{550} \cdot 100 \approx 27.3\%
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.** Halving the menu choices from 8 to 4 reduces reaction time by $\approx 27.3\%$, confirming Hick's Law: **fewer, well-chosen options lead to faster user decisions**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Norman's Seven Stages to Interface Design Failures

| Norman's Stage | Common Interface Failure | Correct HCI Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Goal Formation | User cannot tell what the app *does*. | Onboarding tutorials, clear headline value propositions. |
| Plan Formation | User does not know the *next step*. | Visible breadcrumbs, step indicators ("Step 2 of 4"). |
| Specification | Icons are cryptic and unlabeled. | Familiar metaphors (trash can for delete, floppy disk for save). |
| Execution | Buttons are too small for the target audience. | Apply Fitts' Law — minimum target size of $9$ mm $\times$ $9$ mm for touch. |
| Perception | No visible confirmation of an action. | Hover states, button-press animations, audio cues. |
| Interpretation | Error messages use jargon ("HTTP 500"). | Plain-language errors ("Something went wrong. Please try again."). |
| Evaluation | User cannot tell if the task succeeded. | Confirmation modals, "Undo" buttons, success notifications. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Core HCI Interaction Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the cyclical, closed-loop nature of any human-computer interaction, mapping Norman's Seven Stages of Action onto the physical signal flow between user and machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userNode1["USER SIDE COGNITION"] --> goalNode["Goal Formation High level intent"] 
    goalNode --> planNode["Plan Formation Strategy to achieve goal"] 
    planNode --> specNode["Action Specification Translate plan into motor sequence"] 
    specNode --> execNode["Execution Physical action mouse click keystroke touch gesture"]
    execNode --> interfaceNode["INTERFACE Boundary of translation hardware plus software"]
    interfaceNode --> sysNode["COMPUTER SYSTEM Receives input processes and changes state"]
    sysNode --> outNode["Output Generation Visual audio haptic feedback"]
    outNode --> percNode["Perception Sensory organs detect new state"]
    percNode --> interpNode["Interpretation Brain decodes perceived signals"]
    interpNode --> evalNode["Evaluation Compare perceived outcome to original goal"]
    evalNode --> decisionNode{"Goal Achieved"}
    decisionNode -- Yes --> userNode1
    decisionNode -- No --> specNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **left and top branches** represent the user's *cognition* and *action*. The **bottom and right branches** represent the computer's *response* and the user's *perception*. The two "Gulfs" (Execution and Evaluation) sit precisely at the **interface boundary**, which is why the interface is the most critical engineering component of any HCI system.

### 4.2 Human-Centered Design (HCD) Iterative Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctxNode["1 Understand Context of Use Users Tasks Environments"] --> reqNode["2 Specify User Requirements Functional and Non Functional"]
    reqNode --> desNode["3 Produce Design Solutions Sketches Wireframes Prototypes"]
    desNode --> evalNode["4 Evaluate Against Requirements Usability Testing Heuristics"]
    evalNode --> judgeNode{"Usability Goals Met"}
    judgeNode -- No --> desNode
    judgeNode -- Yes --> shipNode["5 Deploy System and Monitor Real World Usage"]
    shipNode --> ctxNode
```

### 4.3 Mapping Human Sensory Channels to Interface Modalities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    humanBox["HUMAN USER"]
    visualSense["Visual Channel Eyes"] 
    auditorySense["Auditory Channel Ears"] 
    hapticSense["Haptic Channel Skin Proprioception"] 
    motorOut["Motor Output Hands Voice Body"]
    humanBox --> visualSense
    humanBox --> auditorySense
    humanBox --> hapticSense
    humanBox --> motorOut
    visualSense -->|"display"| sysIn1["Screen LEDs Projection"]
    auditorySense -->|"alerts"| sysIn2["Speakers Headphones Bone Conduction"]
    hapticSense -->|"touch"| sysIn3["Vibration Motors Force Feedback Surfaces"]
    motorOut -->|"input"| sysIn4["Keyboard Mouse Touchscreen Microphone Camera"]
    sysIn1 --> computerBox["COMPUTER SYSTEM"]
    sysIn2 --> computerBox
    sysIn3 --> computerBox
    sysIn4 --> computerBox
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Interaction Styles (Evolution of HCI)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level1["1950s Batch Punch Cards"] --> level2["1960s Command Line CLI"]
    level2 --> level3["1970s Menus and Forms"]
    level3 --> level4["1980s WIMP GUI"]
    level4 --> level5["2000s Web 2 0 Multi Touch"]
    level5 --> level6["2010s Mobile First Gestures"]
    level6 --> level7["2020s Conversational AI Voice AR VR Brain Computer Interfaces"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** Each level does not fully replace the previous one. A 2025 user still occasionally uses a CLI, a GUI, and a voice interface **simultaneously**. The HCI engineer's job is to choose the *right interaction style for the right task and the right user*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HCI with "UI design."** UI is one deliverable; HCI is the engineering process that produces it.
> 2. **Fitts' Law units trap:** Movement time is in **milliseconds (ms)**, not seconds, when using the constants $a$ and $b$ in the typical MacKenzie form. Convert at the *end* of the calculation.
> 3. **Norman's Stages are tested in order.** Students often swap the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation. Mnemonic: **E**valuation comes *after* Execution in the alphabet, and *after* action in the timeline.
> 4. **Hick's Law vs. Fitts' Law:** Hick = *decision* time (choosing an option). Fitts = *movement* time (pointing to a target). Examiners love to test this distinction.

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define Human-Computer Interaction. List any four disciplines that contribute to HCI.**

**Model Answer:**
Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a multidisciplinary field concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use and the study of the surrounding phenomena.

The four contributing disciplines are:
1. **Computer Science** — input/output hardware, software architecture, and programming paradigms.
2. **Cognitive Psychology** — human perception, memory, attention, and problem-solving.
3. **Ergonomics / Human Factors Engineering** — physical interaction, posture, and safety.
4. **Design / Industrial Design** — visual aesthetics, interaction metaphors, and user experience.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Explain the "Gulf of Execution" and "Gulf of Evaluation" with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Gulf of Execution:** The gap between the user's *intention* (what they want to do) and the *physical actions* the system allows. A good interface narrows this gulf by making available actions obvious. *Example:* A visible "Save" icon on a word processor clearly tells the user how to save their document.
- **Gulf of Evaluation:** The gap between the system's *actual state* and the user's *understanding* of that state. A good interface narrows this gulf by providing clear, immediate **feedback**. *Example:* After clicking "Send," an email client displays a "Message sent" notification, confirming the action's success.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Question A OR Question B. Both questions cover the same module and carry equal weightage (7 + 7 marks).

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action** with a neat diagram. Discuss how the model helps in identifying interface design problems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Fitts' Law** from its information-theoretic origins. A user must move the cursor $D = 400$ pixels to a target of width $W = 50$ pixels. The fitted constants are $a = 60$ ms and $b = 180$ ms/bit. Compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, **Movement Time (MT)**, and **Throughput (TP)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Stages of Action:**
The seven stages are:
1. **Goal Formation** — The user establishes the high-level objective.
2. **Plan Formation** — The user decides on a strategy.
3. **Action Specification** — The plan is translated into specific motor commands.
4. **Execution** — The user physically performs the action.
5. **Perception** — The user senses the system's response.
6. **Interpretation** — The user decodes the perceived signals.
7. **Evaluation** — The user compares the outcome with the original goal.

The stages are bridged by two critical gaps:
- **Gulf of Execution** (between stages 3 and 4) — bridged by *affordances* and clear control mappings.
- **Gulf of Evaluation** (between stages 5 and 7) — bridged by *feedback*.

This model helps designers diagnose **where** in the interaction cycle a user is failing, allowing targeted remedies (e.g., adding labels to bridge the Gulf of Execution).

**(b) Fitts' Law Derivation and Computation:**

The derivation begins with the Shannon-Hartley theorem:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

By analogy, Fitts mapped $D$ to signal amplitude, $W$ to noise, and $MT$ to inverse bandwidth, yielding:

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Computation with $D = 400$, $W = 50$, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 180$ ms/bit:**

Step 1: Index of Difficulty

$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{400}{50} + 1\right) = \log_{2}(9) \approx 3.17 \text{ bits}
$$

Step 2: Movement Time

$$
MT = 60 + 180 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 400}{50}\right) = 60 + 180 \cdot \log_{2}(16) = 60 + 180 \cdot 4 = 780 \text{ ms}
$$

Step 3: Throughput

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{3.17}{0.780} \approx 4.06 \text{ bits/second}
$$

**Conclusion:** The throughput of $\approx 4.06$ bits/sec falls within the typical human pointing performance range, indicating a moderately efficient interface.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is **Hick's Law**? A navigation menu in a web app has **16** options. Using $a = 200$ ms and $b = 100$ ms, calculate the decision time. If the options are grouped into **4 sub-menus of 4 options each**, calculate the new decision time and the percentage improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Human-Centered Design (HCD) lifecycle** as defined by ISO 9241-210. List the four primary activities and explain why iteration is essential. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hick's Law:**

Hick's Law states that the time taken to make a decision is a logarithmic function of the number of available choices:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)
$$
