```mermaid
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockG
    blockF --> blockH
    blockF --> blockI
    blockG --> blockJ
    blockH --> blockJ
    blockI --> blockJ
    blockG --> blockK
    blockH --> blockK
    blockI --> blockK
    blockJ --> blockD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The **feedback loop** from `blockJ` (Prototype) back to `blockD` (Conceptual Design) represents the *iterative refinement* characteristic of user-centred design. The three evaluation blocks (G, H, I) are *parallel* evaluators that produce *both* design artefacts and quantitative metrics.

## 4.4 Mapping of Affordance Types in Interactive Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1[Affordance]
    nodeA2[Physical Real]
    nodeA3[Perceived Cognitive]
    nodeA4[Learnable Cultural]
    nodeA5[Examples]
    nodeA6[Button shape invites press]
    nodeA7[Drag handle suggests drag]
    nodeA8[Folder icon implies storage]
    nodeA9[Red color culturally means stop]

    nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    nodeA1 --> nodeA3
    nodeA1 --> nodeA4
    nodeA2 --> nodeA5
    nodeA3 --> nodeA5
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6
    nodeA5 --> nodeA7
    nodeA5 --> nodeA8
    nodeA5 --> nodeA9
```

## 4.5 Interaction Styles Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIS[Interaction Styles]
    nodeIS1[Command Line]
    nodeIS2[Menu Driven]
    nodeIS3[Form Fillin]
    nodeIS4[Direct Manipulation]
    nodeIS5[Natural Language]
    nodeIS6[VR AR Gesture]

    nodeIS --> nodeIS1
    nodeIS --> nodeIS2
    nodeIS --> nodeIS3
    nodeIS --> nodeIS4
    nodeIS --> nodeIS5
    nodeIS --> nodeIS6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Cue:** When asked to *compare* interaction styles, use this hierarchy and emphasize that **Direct Manipulation** (e.g., dragging a file to the trash) is generally superior for novices because it leverages **Norman-style affordances** and reduces **short-term memory load** (Shneiderman's Rule 8).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Affordance" in the context of interactive design. Differentiate between real and perceived affordances with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An **affordance**, as defined by psychologist James J. Gibson and popularized in HCI by Don Norman, is a **property of an object that suggests how it can be used by an agent**.
>
> - **Real Affordance** is the *actual* physical property of an object that enables an action. Example: A **door handle** that is shaped like a lever actually *affords* pushing down. The shape permits a downward press.
> - **Perceived Affordance** is the *user's perception* of what actions are possible, which may or may not match the real affordance. Example: A **flat plate** on a door *looks like* it should be pushed (perceived push), but the real affordance may be a pull (it has a *signifier* gap).
>
> Good design ensures that the **perceived affordance matches the real affordance** through clear **signifiers** (1 mark each for definition, real example, perceived example — total 3 marks).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three of Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules of Interface Design and briefly explain each in one sentence.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per rule):**
>
> 1. **Strive for Consistency** — Identical actions, words, and situations should be represented by identical visual elements and sequences throughout the interface (e.g., the same "Save" icon everywhere).
> 2. **Offer Informative Feedback** — For every operator action, the system should provide a visible, audible, or tactile response at a level appropriate to the action (e.g., a button depresses when clicked, a progress bar advances during download).
> 3. **Permit Easy Reversal of Actions** — Provide an "Undo" function for almost every user action, reducing anxiety and encouraging exploration (e.g., Ctrl+Z in word processors, the Gmail "Undo Send" toast).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain Don Norman's **Seven Principles of Design** in detail with suitable examples for each principle. (7 marks)

> **Model Answer (7 marks — 1 mark per principle, 7th mark for overall coherence/examples):**
>
> Don Norman, in *The Design of Everyday Things* (originally *The Psychology of Everyday Things*, 1988), articulated **seven principles** that govern how well a designed object supports the user's understanding and action. The seven principles are:
>
> 1. **Discoverability**: The user should be able to *discover* what actions are possible and the current state of the system just by looking. *Example:* A touchscreen kiosk at an airport has visibly lit buttons indicating "Tap to begin" — the user immediately knows an action is possible.
>
> 2. **Feedback**: The system must continuously inform the user about the result of their actions, in a timely and visible manner. *Example:* When a file is deleted, the OS moves it to a Recycle Bin and the bin icon updates its count, confirming the action.
>
> 3. **Conceptual Model**: The system should project a clear, consistent, and intuitive *mental image* of how it works. *Example:* The desktop metaphor (folders, files, trash can) gives the user a clear conceptual model that maps to real-world objects.
>
> 4. **Affordances**: The design should suggest appropriate actions; perceived affordances must match the actual affordances. *Example:* A push-button on a microwave *affords* pressing; a sliding switch *affords* sliding.
>
> 5. **Signifiers**: Effective *signals* (visible, audible, tactile) must communicate where the action should take place. *Example:* A label "Press to start," a glow on a touch sensor, or a raised texture on a handle.
>
> 6. **Mappings**: The relationship between controls and their effects should be natural, spatial, or culturally obvious. *Example:* The stove burner knob is spatially mapped — the leftmost knob controls the leftmost burner.
>
> 7. **Constraints**: Physical, logical, semantic, and cultural constraints should guide the user and prevent errors. *Example:* A PIN-entry field on an ATM *constrains* input to 4 digits and accepts only numerics, preventing typing letters.
>
> **Synthesis:** Norman's principles collectively describe how design *minimises the gulf of execution* (between user intent and physical action) and *the gulf of evaluation* (between system state and user perception). A good interactive system closes both gulfs through clear discoverability, feedback, and natural mapping.

#### (b) Describe the **iterative design lifecycle** of an interactive system. Why is iteration essential in HCI design? List at least **three evaluation methods** used in each iteration. (7 marks)

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> The **iterative design lifecycle** is a user-centred approach in which design, prototyping, and evaluation are performed in **repeated cycles** until the system meets the target usability goals. The classical phases of one iteration are:
>
> **(1) Requirements Gathering** (1 mark): Identify user needs, task requirements, and domain constraints through interviews, questionnaires, observation, and document analysis.
>
> **(2) Analysis and Modeling** (1 mark): Build task models, use-case diagrams, and persona profiles. Identify potential usability issues early.
>
> **(3) Design Phase**: Generate alternative conceptual designs (paper sketches, wireframes, storyboards). Choose the most promising concept.
>
> **(4) Prototype Phase**: Build an interactive prototype (low-fidelity or high-fidelity) that can be tested with users.
>
> **(5) Evaluation Phase**: Test the prototype with users and collect both qualitative and quantitative data.
>
> **(6) Refinement**: Address identified issues and feed them back into the next iteration.
>
> **Why Iteration is Essential** (2 marks):
> - **Continuous User Feedback:** Users cannot articulate all requirements upfront; iteration reveals latent needs.
> - **Early Bug Detection:** Errors caught in iteration 1 cost roughly 10× less to fix than in final deployment.
> - **Technology Evolution:** Hardware, OS, and user expectations evolve during a project's lifetime.
> - **Risk Reduction:** Each iteration de-risks a portion of the design; the final product is much more predictable.
>
> **Three Evaluation Methods Used in Each Iteration** (2 marks — 1 mark for naming + 1 mark for brief explanation):
>
> 1. **Heuristic Evaluation** — A small set of expert evaluators (typically 3–5) inspects the interface against Nielsen's 10 heuristics. Each evaluator independently flags violations; findings are aggregated. It is *cheap, fast, and requires no users*, making it ideal for early iterations.
>
> 2. **Cognitive Walkthrough** — A structured review in which one or more evaluators simulate a user working through a sequence of actions to achieve a goal, focusing on whether the user can *discover* the correct action, *understand* feedback, and *progress* toward the goal. It targets *learnability* for first-time users.
>
> 3. **User Testing (Think-Aloud Protocol)** — Real representative users are asked to perform realistic tasks on the prototype while *verbalizing* their thoughts. The evaluator notes where the user hesitates, makes errors, or asks for help. This yields *rich qualitative data* and is considered the *gold standard* for usability evaluation.
>
> *Optional additional methods:* Pluralistic Walkthrough, Feature Inspection, Formal Usability Testing (with metrics), Heuristic Evaluation with GOMS, A/B testing.

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain **Nielsen's Ten Usability Heuristics** in detail. How are they used in a *heuristic evaluation*? (7 marks)

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> Jakob Nielsen, in 1994, distilled Molich's 1990 heuristics into **10 widely-adopted usability principles** that serve as a *checklist* for evaluating interactive systems. The heuristics are:
>
> 1. **Visibility of System Status**: Keep users informed about what is going on through timely feedback. *Example:* A progress bar during file upload.
>
> 2. **Match Between System and the Real World**: Use the user's language, follow real-world conventions, and present information in a natural order. *Example:* A calendar icon (📅) to open a date-picker.
>
> 3. **User Control and Freedom**: Provide undo/redo and clearly marked emergency exits. *Example:* "Cancel" button during a long installation.
>
> 4. **Consistency and Standards**: Follow platform conventions; same words/situations should mean the same thing. *Example:* "OK" button always on the right.
>
> 5. **Error Prevention**: Design carefully to prevent errors from occurring. *Example:* Disabling the "Send" button until all required fields are filled.
>
> 6. **Recognition Rather than Recall**: Make objects, actions, and options visible. *Example:* Visible menu items rather than hidden commands.
>
> 7. **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use**: Provide accelerators (shortcuts) for expert users while keeping the interface usable for novices. *Example:* Keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+S, Ctrl+C).
>
> 8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design**: Avoid irrelevant information. *Example:* A clean checkout page with only essential fields.
>
> 9. **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors**: Error messages should be in plain language, indicate the problem, and suggest a solution. *Example:* "Password must be at least 8 characters" instead of "Error 0x4A2."
>
> 10. **Help and Documentation**: Provide task-focused, searchable help. *Example:* The F1 help in Microsoft Office.
>
> **How They Are Used in Heuristic Evaluation** (1 mark):
> In a **heuristic evaluation session**, **3 to 5 evaluators** independently walk through the interface twice — once to *get a feel for the system* and once to *focus on specific heuristics*. Each evaluator flags **usability violations**, rates their **severity** (0 = not a usability problem, 4 = usability catastrophe), and proposes **fixes**. The findings are then aggregated, deduplicated, and prioritised for the next design iteration. The method is **discount** (cheap) and can be performed *without real users*, making it ideal for early-stage prototypes. (1 mark for severity scale, 1 mark for procedure, 1 mark for evaluator count, 1 mark for aggregation.)

#### (b) Discuss the **quantitative usability metrics** — Effectiveness, Efficiency, Learnability, Error Rate, and SUS Score — with formulas, units, and an example for each. (7 marks)

> **Model Answer (7 marks — ~1.4 marks per metric):**
>
> 1. **Effectiveness** — Measures *whether* the user can complete a task. (1 mark)
>
> $$E = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Tasks Attempted}} \times 100\%$$
>
> *Unit:* Percentage (0–100%). *Example:* 42 out of 50 users completed the checkout successfully → $E = 84\%$.
>
> 2. **Efficiency** — Measures *how quickly* a task can be completed. (1 mark)
>
> $$\text{Eff} = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Time Taken (s)}}$$
>
> *Unit:* Tasks per second. *Example:* A trained user completes 8 searches in 2 minutes → $\text{Eff} = 8/120 \approx 0.067$ tasks/s.
>
> 3. **Learnability** — Measures *how quickly* a first-time user reaches expert performance. (1 mark)
>
> $$L = \frac{\text{Time taken by first-time user}}{\text{Time taken by expert user}}$$
>
> *Unit:* Dimensionless ratio. *Example:* A first-time user takes 120 s; an expert takes 30 s → $L = 4$. A *lower* $L$ means *better* learnability.
>
> 4. **Error Rate** — Measures the frequency of errors per attempt. (1 mark)
>
> $$R_{\text{err}} = \frac{\text{Number of Errors}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \times 100\%$$
>
> *Unit:* Percentage. *Example:* 12 errors in 100 password entries → $R_{\text{err}} = 12\%$.
>
> 5. **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — A standardized 10-item 5-point Likert questionnaire yielding a 0–100 score. (2 marks)
>
> $$\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \left[ \sum_{i \in \text{odd}} (R_i - 1) + \sum_{i \in \text{even}} (5 - R_i) \right]$$
>
> *Unit:* Score from 0 to 100. A score **> 68** is considered above-average usability. *Example:* After 10 users complete the SUS, the average score is **74.75** — indicating a *good* interface.
>
> **Closing remark (1 mark):** These five metrics together form a *quantitative usability profile*. An ideal interface should *maximise* Effectiveness, Efficiency, and SUS, and *minimise* the Learnability ratio and Error Rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Interactive Design Basics questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Norman and Shneiderman:** Many students *blend* Norman's principles with Shneiderman's rules, losing credit for lack of attribution. *Always* cite the author **by name** before listing the rules.
>
> 2. **Listing without examples:** The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **30–40%** of the marks for *examples*. A list of 8 rules with no example fetches at most **3 out of 7 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing affordance with signifier:** A button that *looks* pressable is a **signifier**; the *act* of pressing is the **affordance**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Formula errors in SUS:** Forgetting the **2.5×** multiplier is the most common error. *Tip:* Memorize the per-item contribution rules — odd items use $(R - 1)$ and even items use $(5 - R)$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the explanation of *why* iteration is essential:** In a 7-mark "iterative design" question, merely *listing* the phases scores only 3 marks. The remaining 4 marks are for *justification* of iteration's necessity.
>
> 6. **Not using units in formulas:** Always accompany a metric formula with its *unit* and a *range of acceptable values* (e.g., SUS = 0–100, Effectiveness = 0–100%).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Interactive Design Basics**
>
> ☐ **Norman's 7 Principles** — *D-F-C-A-S-M-C*: Discoverability, Feedback, Conceptual Model, Affordances, Signifiers, Mappings, Constraints. Use the **gulf of execution** and **gulf of evaluation** as the analytical backbone.
>
> ☐ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** — 1987, *prescriptive*. Always state them as imperatives ("Strive for…", "Enable…"). Mnemonic: *ConS, Short, Feed, Close, Error, Undo, Control, Memory*.
>
> ☐ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — 1994, *evaluative*. H1–H10 are the standard checklist for **heuristic evaluation**. Recommended evaluator count = **3 to 5**.
>
> ☐ **Heuristic Evaluation Procedure** — Each evaluator does **2 passes**; rate severity on a **0–4** scale; aggregate findings; prioritise by severity.
>
> ☐ **Iterative Lifecycle** — Requirements → Analysis → Design → Prototype → Evaluate → Refine. The **feedback loop** from evaluation back to design is the defining feature.
>
> ☐ **Five Quantitative Metrics**:
>    • Effectiveness = success / attempts × 100%
>    • Efficiency = success / time
>    • Learnability = $T_{\text{novice}} / T_{\text{expert}}$ (lower is better)
>    • Error Rate = errors / attempts × 100%
>    • SUS = $2.5 \times \sum (\text{odd: }R-1) + (\text{even: }5-R)$; benchmark = **68**.
>
> ☐ **GOMS Constants** — $K = 0.1$ s, $P = 1.2$ s, $M = 1.35$ s, $R = 0.23$ s (Card, Moran, Newell).
>
> ☐ **Core Distinctions**:
>    • **Affordance vs. Signifier** — Affordance is the *action possibility*; signifier is the *perceivable cue* communicating it.
>    • **Recognition vs. Recall** (Nielsen H6) — Interfaces should let users *recognize* options, not *recall* them.
>    • **Effectiveness vs. Efficiency** — Effectiveness = *can they do it?*; Efficiency = *how fast can they do it?*
>
> ☐ **Always** cite the *author* (Norman / Shneiderman / Nielsen) when listing the rules.
>
> ☐ **Always** give a *real-world example* (ATM, MS Word, Google, iPhone) to maximise marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Human Machine Interfaces (HMI)**, a **Process** refers to the structured, sequential, and often continuous set of operations, transformations, or activities — either physical, chemical, computational, or informational — that an HMI system is designed to **monitor, supervise, control, and visualize**. A process is the *underlying reality* that the human operator interacts with *through* the interface.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT724)** syllabus perspective, a process is characterized by:

1. **Inputs (Independent Variables):** Raw materials, energy, data, or user commands entering the system.
2. **Transformation Mechanism:** The physical/chemical/logical operations acting upon the inputs.
3. **Outputs (Dependent Variables):** The desired products, results, or feedback signals.
4. **State Variables:** Time-dependent measurable parameters (temperature, pressure, flow rate, voltage, position, etc.).
5. **Control Parameters:** Setpoints and tuning variables manipulated by the operator via the HMI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, the term "Process" is treated as the **central object of abstraction** — the HMI is essentially a *graphical, interactive façade* placed over a process so that a human can perceive, understand, predict, and manipulate it without physical immersion in hazardous or complex environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in the cockpit of a modern commercial airliner at 35,000 feet. The actual *process* — combustion in engines, air pressure differentials over the wings, fuel flow through micro-tubes, navigation calculations — is happening hundreds of meters away from you. You cannot touch the engine or feel the air. What you *can* do is glance at screens (PFD, ND, ECAM), turn knobs, push buttons, and speak into a microphone.

- The **engines, wings, fuel lines, and flight computers** = the **Process**.
- The **screens, knobs, buttons, and audio alerts** = the **HMI**.
- **You** = the human operator in the loop.

The HMI translates invisible physical phenomena into glass-display symbols (e.g., a rising altitude tape, a green arc on the airspeed indicator) and translates your hand movements into electrical signals reaching the process. The *process* is the **subject**; the HMI is the **lens** through which you see and shape it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A *process* in HMI is the "thing happening in the world" (boiling water, cutting metal, routing packets, dispensing medicine) that you supervise from behind a screen. The HMI is your *window* and *control panel* into that thing.

### 1.3 Classification of Processes (KTU Perspective)

| Process Type | Description | HMI Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Process** | Runs indefinitely with steady-state variables | Oil refinery, power plant SCADA |
| **Batch Process** | Discrete start–end cycles with defined recipes | Pharmaceutical mixing, bakery oven |
| **Discrete Process** | Production of countable units | Automotive assembly line |
| **Hybrid Process** | Combination of continuous and discrete | Bottling plant (continuous filling + discrete capping) |
| **Computational/Informational Process** | Data transformations, transactions | Banking Kiosk, Air Traffic Control console |

### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Process Variable (PV):** A measurable quantity representing process state (e.g., $T, P, F, L$ for temperature, pressure, flow, level).
- **Setpoint (SP):** The desired target value of a PV.
- **Controlled Variable (CV):** The PV that the controller acts to maintain at SP.
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** The variable adjusted by the controller (valve position, motor speed).
- **Disturbance (D):** Unwanted input that drives the process away from SP.
- **Process Lag / Dead Time ($\theta_d$):** Delay between an input change and observable response.
- **Process Time Constant ($\tau$):** Time taken to reach ~63.2% of a step change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Recap:** The **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** model is the canonical KTU-recommended abstraction for any single-loop process, expressed by the parameters **$K_p$ (process gain), $\tau$ (time constant), and $\theta_d$ (dead time)**. These three numbers are usually sufficient for an HMI designer to decide the *update interval*, *alarm thresholds*, and *control panel complexity*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Process in the HMI Loop

A process, when viewed through an HMI, can be decomposed into **five functional layers**:

1. **Physical Layer** — Sensors (transducers) and actuators converting physical ↔ electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning Layer** — Amplifiers, filters, A/D and D/A converters.
3. **Communication Layer** — Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, Modbus TCP, EtherCAT) carrying data between the process and the HMI computer.
4. **Supervisory Layer** — SCADA/HMI software that *models* the process using tags, alarms, trends, and mimic diagrams.
5. **Operator Layer** — The human who interprets the model and issues commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** The HMI does not *control* the process directly — it represents the process symbolically so that a human (who is slow, intelligent, and adaptive) can act as the highest-level decision-maker in the loop.

### 2.2 Process Models Used in HMI Design

To design an HMI that genuinely supports the operator, the engineer must mathematically or diagrammatically model the process. The KTU curriculum recognizes three foundational approaches:

#### 2.2.1 The FOPDT Transfer Function Model
For a vast majority of industrial processes, a first-order linear approximation suffices:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where:
- $K_p$ = steady-state process gain (dimensionless ratio of output to input change)
- $\tau$ = process time constant (seconds)
- $\theta_d$ = dead time / transport delay (seconds)
- $s$ = Laplace operator

The **step response** $y(t)$ to a step input of magnitude $\Delta u$ is:

$$y(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \left(1 - e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau}\right) \quad \text{for } t > \theta_d$$
$$y(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t \leq \theta_d$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When the ratio $\theta_d / \tau > 1$, the process is called **"dead-time dominant"** and requires **specialized HMI features** such as predictive trend displays, Smith-Predictor visualizations, and operator confidence indicators — because the operator's intuitive mental model of "I turned the knob → something should happen *now*" breaks down.

#### 2.2.2 The State-Space Model
For multivariable, coupled processes (e.g., a chemical reactor where temperature and concentration are interdependent):

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)$$
$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ = state vector (internal process variables)
- $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ = input vector (manipulated variables)
- $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ = output vector (measured variables)
- $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ = matrices representing process dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why State-Space Matters in HMI:** State-space exposes *internal variables* that are not directly measurable. The HMI can use a **Soft Sensor (Observer)** to display estimated values (e.g., inferred product quality) to the operator.

#### 2.2.3 The Task-Process (Cognitive) Model — KLM/GOMS
At the **user-cognition layer**, a *process* is also the sequence of cognitive and motor steps a human performs. The **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** by Card, Moran & Newell predicts task completion time:

$$T_{\text{task}} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$$

| Operator Symbol | Physical Operator Description | KTU Standard Time (seconds) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_K$ | Keystroke (physical key press) | **0.28 s** (expert), 0.2–1.2 s (novice) |
| $T_P$ | Pointing (mouse to target) | **1.10 s** |
| $T_H$ | Homing (hand between devices) | **0.40 s** |
| $T_D$ | Drawing (line segment) | varies (≈ 0.9 × length in cm) |
| $T_M$ | Mental preparation (cognitive) | **1.20 s** |
| $T_R$ | System response wait | depends on process |

### 2.3 Process Representation in HMI

The process is *abstracted* into HMI artifacts:

| HMI Artifact | Process Element Represented |
|---|---|
| **Mimic Diagram** | Spatial/topological layout of equipment |
| **Trend Chart** | Time evolution of PVs |
| **Alarm List** | Exception states of the process |
| **Faceplate** | Detailed view of a single control loop (PID block) |
| **Recipe / SFC View** | Sequential function chart of batch steps |
| **Overview Dashboard** | Aggregate KPIs of the process |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the FOPDT step response and the KLM equation. These appear in nearly every KTU HMI question paper as a 7-mark sub-part.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description / Use | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | FOPDT process model | **Very High** |
| 2 | $y(t) = K_p \Delta u \left(1 - e^{-(t-\theta_d)/\tau}\right)$ | FOPDT step response | **Very High** |
| 3 | $T_{63.2} = \tau$ | Time to reach 63.2% of final value | High |
| 4 | $T_{settle} \approx 4\tau$ | 2% settling time | High |
| 5 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ | State-space dynamics | Medium |
| 6 | $T_{\text{task}} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$ | KLM cognitive task time | **Very High** |
| 7 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{repair},i}$ | Mean Time To Repair (process reliability metric) | Medium |
| 8 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | Medium |
| 9 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard rate / failure intensity | Low |
| 10 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability index | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The FOPDT model is the *lingua franca* of control engineers. A correct HMI trend-update interval, for example, is typically chosen as $\Delta t_{\text{update}} \leq \tau / 5$ to satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion** for visual smoothness.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Process Modeling in HMI

- **Predictability:** A modeled process lets the HMI *forecast* future states and warn the operator *before* a deviation occurs (predictive alarms).
- **Transparency:** A well-modeled process shown via a mimic diagram makes the system *legible* — the operator can build an accurate **mental model** (Norman, 1988).
- **Trust Calibration:** If the HMI's representation matches the true process dynamics, the operator builds **appropriate trust**; if it doesn't, *automation surprise* occurs.
- **Training:** Process models drive high-fidelity simulators used to train operators without risking real equipment.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: FOPDT Step Response

**Problem Setup:** A temperature process has $K_p = 2.5\ ^\circ\text{C}/\%$, $\tau = 60\ \text{s}$, and $\theta_d = 10\ \text{s}$. A step change of $\Delta u = 10\%$ is applied to the heater. Derive the explicit expression for $y(t)$ and compute the value at $t = 70\ \text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing differential equation.**

The FOPDT model in time domain, ignoring dead time initially, is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K_p \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time delay.**

For a step input $u(t) = \Delta u \cdot \mathbf{1}(t - \theta_d)$ where $\mathbf{1}(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \mathbf{1}(t - \theta_d)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous part.**

The homogeneous equation $\tau \dot{y} + y = 0$ yields the characteristic root $s = -1/\tau$, giving:

$$y_h(t) = C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Find a particular solution for $t > \theta_d$.**

In steady state, $\dot{y} = 0$, so $y_p = K_p \cdot \Delta u$.

**Step 5 — Apply continuity of $y$ at $t = \theta_d$.**

For $t < \theta_d$, $u = 0$, so $y = 0$. Thus, at $t = \theta_d^+$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = C \cdot e^{-\theta_d/\tau} + K_p \Delta u \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -K_p \Delta u \cdot e^{\theta_d/\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the complete solution.**

$$y(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \left[1 - e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau}\right] \quad \text{for } t \geq \theta_d$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$y(70) = 2.5 \times 10 \times \left[1 - e^{-(70 - 10)/60}\right]$$

$$y(70) = 25 \times \left[1 - e^{-1}\right]$$

$$y(70) = 25 \times \left[1 - 0.3679\right]$$

$$y(70) = 25 \times 0.6321$$

$$y(70) \approx 15.80\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Interpretation:** At $t = 70\ \text{s}$ (i.e., one time-constant after the dead time elapsed), the temperature has risen to **63.2% of its final value**, exactly matching the FOPDT definition. This is the canonical "tuning point" HMI designers use to place a *first-warning* alarm threshold.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: KLM Task Time

**Problem:** A control-room operator must (i) mentally decide to acknowledge an alarm ($M$), (ii) move the mouse to the "ACK" button ($P$), (iii) click it ($K$), and (iv) wait for the system response ($R = 0.8\ \text{s}$). Estimate total task time using the KLM formula.

**Step 1 — List the operators in sequence:**

| Sequence | Operator | Duration (s) |
|---|---|---|
| Mental preparation | $T_M$ | 1.20 |
| Point to ACK button | $T_P$ | 1.10 |
| Click | $T_K$ | 0.28 |
| System response | $T_R$ | 0.80 |
| **Total** | — | **3.38 s** |

**Step 2 — Apply the KLM equation:**

$$T_{\text{task}} = T_M + T_P + T_K + T_R = 1.20 + 1.10 + 0.28 + 0.80 = 3.38\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the KLM heuristic "M before K" rule:**

The mental operator $T_M$ is *preparatory* and is therefore placed *before* the physical operators but not *between* them. This is the canonical CPM-GOMS ordering.

**Interpretation:** If the alarm must be acknowledged within **2 seconds** (e.g., a nuclear plant scram alarm), the operator will *fail* the task using a mouse-driven HMI. The KTU-rigorous design recommendation: provide a **dedicated hardware ACK button** ($T_K \approx 0.08\ \text{s}$) on the console to reduce $T_{\text{task}}$ to ≈ 2.18 s.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables vs. HMI Display Choices

| Process Characteristic | Quantitative Indicator | Recommended HMI Display Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Fast dynamics ($\tau < 1\ \text{s}$) | $\tau$ small | High-refresh trend (≥ 10 Hz), numeric big-font readout |
| Slow dynamics ($\tau > 60\ \text{s}$) | $\tau$ large | Compressed-time trend, focus on setpoint tracking |
| Large dead time ($\theta_d / \tau > 1$) | Smith predictor | Predictive trajectory overlay, "what-if" sliders |
| Multiple coupled variables | Off-diagonal $\mathbf{A}_{ij}$ | Multi-variable radar / parallel-trend overlay |
| Safety-critical state | High severity index | Hard-wired physical E-Stop, redundant visual + audible alarm |
| Operator cognitive bottleneck | KLM $T_{\text{task}} >$ deadline | Reduce mental operators, increase automation |
| High variability ($\sigma$ large) | $C_p < 1$ | SPC charts (control charts) on HMI |
| Batch / sequential | Recipe steps | SFC (Sequential Function Chart) view with step highlighting |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The HMI–Process Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[Operator / Human] -->|Commands| HMI[HMI Software Layer]
    HMI -->|Control Signals| PLC[PLC / RTU Controller]
    PLC -->|Actuator Drive| ACT[Actuators Valves Motors]
    ACT -->|Physical Action| PROC[Industrial Process Plant]
    PROC -->|Measured Variables| SENS[Sensors Transducers]
    SENS -->|Analog Digital| PLC
    PLC -->|Process Data Tags| HMI
    HMI -->|Visual Auditory Feedback| OP
    SENS -.->|Hardwired Safety Path| ESD[Emergency Shutdown]
    ESD -.->|Trip Signal| ACT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Follow the loop clockwise. The operator issues commands to the HMI; the HMI forwards them to the PLC; the PLC drives actuators; the actuators act on the physical process; sensors measure the result; the PLC reads the sensors; data flows back to the HMI; the operator perceives the updated state. The dashed red lines form the **independent safety layer (ESD)** that bypasses the HMI entirely in emergencies — a fundamental KTU design principle.

---

### 4.2 FOPDT Process Step Response (Conceptual Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process at t = 0: Step Input Applied] --> B[t < theta_d: DEAD TIME]
    B --> C[t = theta_d: Output begins to rise]
    C --> D[t = theta_d + tau: 63.2 percent of final value]
    D --> E[t = theta_d + 4 tau: 98 percent settled]
    E --> F[Steady State Reached]
    style B fill:#ffcccc
    style C fill:#fff4cc
    style D fill:#ccffcc
    style E fill:#ccccff
    style F fill:#e0e0e0
```

---

### 4.3 Process State-Space Representation in HMI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer
        U1[MV1: Valve Position] --> SS
        U2[MV2: Heater Power] --> SS
    end
    subgraph StateSpaceProcess
        SS[Process Dynamics Matrix A B C D] --> X1

        SS --> X3
    end
    X1[x1: Temperature] --> Y1

    X3[x3: Pressure] --> Y3
    Y1[Output y1] --> HMIDisp[HMI Mimic Display]
    Y2[Output y2] --> HMIDisp
    Y3[Output y3] --> HMIDisp
    HMIDisp --> OPFace[Operator Console]
```

### 4.4 HMI Process Representation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    L1 --> L2[Level 2: Area Mimic Diagram]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The **5-level (or 4-level) ISA-95 hierarchy** of process representation is a frequently-tested diagram. Be prepared to label each level and justify why information is *aggregated* as one moves upward — this directly supports the operator's **situation awareness** (Endsley's SA model).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a "Process" in the context of Human Machine Interfaces. List any four process variables with examples.**
*(Model Answer, 3 marks)*

A **Process**, in HMI terminology, is the real-world sequence of physical, chemical, or computational operations that the human operator monitors and controls *through* the interface. It is characterized by inputs, transformations, outputs, and measurable state variables.

**Four process variables:**

| Variable | Symbol | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $T$ | Reactor outlet $^\circ$C |
| Pressure | $P$ | Boiler steam pressure (bar) |
| Flow rate | $F$ | Coolant flow (L/min) |
| Level | $L$ | Tank level (%) |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tabulated variables with correct examples.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is meant by a "dead-time dominant" process? Why is it significant for HMI design?**
*(Model Answer, 3 marks)*

A process is termed **dead-time dominant** when the transport delay $\theta_d$ is greater than the process time constant $\tau$ (i.e., $\theta_d / \tau > 1$).

**Significance for HMI design:**

1. The operator's **direct cause-effect mental model** fails — actions do not produce immediate visible effects, leading to *automation surprise* and *operator mistrust*.
2. The HMI must compensate using **predictive trend overlays**, **confidence indicators**, and **feed-forward visualizations** of expected response trajectories.
3. Alarms must be **delay-compensated**; otherwise, transient alarm floods occur immediately after every setpoint change.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for HMI design implications.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, Module 2]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **FOPDT (First-Order Plus Dead Time) model** of an industrial process. Derive the step response and show that the output reaches 63.2% of its final value at $t = \theta_d + \tau$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A chemical reactor has $K_p = 3.0$, $\tau = 45\ \text{s}$, and $\theta_d = 8\ \text{s}$. A step input of magnitude $\Delta u = 5\%$ is applied at $t=0$. Calculate the output at $t = 53\ \text{s}$ and the **2% settling time**. Comment on the choice of HMI trend refresh interval. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the FOPDT structure.** *(1 mark)*

The FOPDT model approximates a wide class of single-loop processes by combining a first-order lag with a pure time delay. In the Laplace domain:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Write the time-domain ODE.** *(1 mark)*

For an input $u(t)$ applied at $t=0$:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K_p \cdot u(t - \theta_d)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous equation.** *(1 mark)*

The characteristic equation $\tau r + 1 = 0$ gives $r = -1/\tau$. Hence the homogeneous solution is:

$$y_h(t) = C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the particular (steady-state) solution for a step input.** *(1 mark)*

For $u(t - \theta_d) = \Delta u \cdot \mathbf{1}(t - \theta_d)$ and $t \to \infty$:

$$y(\infty) = K_p \cdot \Delta u$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial condition at $t = \theta_d^+$.** *(1 mark)*

Since the output cannot jump (first-order), $y(\theta_d^+) = 0$:

$$0 = C \cdot e^{-\theta_d/\tau} + K_p \Delta u \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -K_p \Delta u \cdot e^{\theta_d/\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble and simplify.** *(1 mark)*

$$y(t) = K_p \Delta u \left[1 - e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau}\right] \quad \text{for } t \geq \theta_d$$

**Step 7 — Prove 63.2% point.** *(1 mark)*

At $t = \theta_d + \tau$:

$$y(\theta_d + \tau) = K_p \Delta u \left[1 - e^{-1}\right] = K_p \Delta u \cdot (1 - 0.3679) = 0.6321 \cdot K_p \Delta u$$

This is exactly **63.2% of the final steady-state value**, $\blacksquare$.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Write the step response formula.** *(1 mark)*

$$y(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \left[1 - e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** *(1 mark)*

$$y(53) = 3.0 \times 5 \times \left[1 - e^{-(53 - 8)/45}\right]$$

$$y(53) = 15 \times \left[1 - e^{-1}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential.** *(1 mark)*

$$e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

$$y(53) = 15 \times 0.6321 \approx 9.48$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 2% settling time.** *(1 mark)*

The 2% settling time is defined as the time at which:

$$y(t) = 0.98 \cdot y(\infty)$$

Setting $1 - e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau} = 0.98$:

$$e^{-(t - \theta_d)/\tau} = 0.02$$

$$(t - \theta_d)/\tau = -\ln(0.02) = 3.912$$

$$t_{settle} = \theta_d + 3.912\tau = 8 + 3.912 \times 45 = 8 + 176.04 = 184.04\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the HMI trend refresh interval.** *(1 mark)*

Following the Nyquist–Shannon visual sampling rule:

$$\Delta t_{\text{update}} \leq \frac{\tau}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9\ \text{s}$$

A **1-second refresh** is recommended for smoothness; **5-second refresh** is the minimum acceptable.

**Step 6 — Comment on HMI design implications.** *(2 marks)*

- The **total screen update cycle** of the HMI must be ≪ 9 s, otherwise the trend will appear "jumpy" and the operator may *misinterpret* the dynamics.
- The **alarm threshold** for deviation from setpoint should be set at $\pm 0.632 \cdot K_p \cdot \Delta u = \pm 9.48$ units for a "first-warn" alert, because this is the natural inflection point of the response.
- The **dead time of 8 s** must be clearly indicated on the HMI faceplate (e.g., a "predictive delay" overlay) to prevent the operator from over-correcting during the silent period.
- The **2% settling time of ≈ 184 s** should be displayed in a "Control Performance" dashboard tile so the operator knows when the process is truly stable.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-layer ISA-95 hierarchy** of process representation in HMI systems. Explain the principle of information aggregation as one moves from Level 5 to Level 1. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** of Card, Moran and Newell, estimate the time taken by an operator to perform the following sub-tasks during an alarm: (i) mentally recognize the alarm ($M$), (ii) move hand from keyboard to mouse ($H$), (iii) point to ACK button ($P$), (iv) click ($K$), (v) mentally verify acknowledgment ($M$), and (vi) wait for system response ($R = 0.5\ \text{s}$). If the regulatory deadline is **2.5 s**, suggest **two HMI design improvements** to meet it. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the ISA-95 framework purpose.** *(1 mark)*

ISA-95 (ANSI/ISA-95, also adopted as IEC 62264) is the international standard for integrating **enterprise (ERP)** systems with **process control (PCS)** systems. For HMI design, it prescribes a five-level hierarchy of process representation, balancing information density with cognitive load.

**Step 2 — Draw/describe the block diagram.** *(2 marks)*

The five levels, from the *physical process* (Level 0) up to the *enterprise* (Level 4), are:

| Level | Name | Function in HMI Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | The physical process itself | Sensors and actuators |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control (PLC/RTU) | Setpoint commands, interlocks |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory (SCADA / HMI) | Mimic diagrams, alarms, trends |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations (MES) | Batch tracking, recipes |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise (ERP) | Business KPIs, scheduling |

**Step 3 — Define the aggregation principle.** *(1 mark)*

As one moves **upward** (Level 0 → 4), data from many lower-level tags are *aggregated* into fewer, higher-level indicators. For example, **1000 raw temperature readings** at Level 0 become **1 plant-average temperature** at Level 2, and finally a **single OEE percentage** at Level 4.

**Step 4 — Explain cognitive rationale.** *(1 mark)*

The aggregation principle exists because the **human operator's working memory can hold only 4 ± 1 chunks** (Miller's Law). Lower-level details (raw tag values) are too numerous to perceive at once; higher-level summaries fit cognition but lack the resolution needed for direct control. The HMI hierarchy provides *progressive disclosure* — the operator drills down as required.

**Step 5 — Justify with a real-world example.** *(1 mark)*

In a power plant, the boiler (Level 0) has 50 sensors. These are presented to the operator as a **mimic diagram of the boiler** (Level 2). At the SCADA overview, the 50 sensors become a **single "Boiler Health" indicator** (Level 4), with a green/yellow/red color code.

**Step 6 — Link to Endsley's SA Model.** *(1 mark)*

The hierarchy directly supports **Endsley's three-level Situation Awareness**: Level 2 = Perception, Level 3 = Comprehension, Level 4 = Projection. A well-designed HMI navigates the operator smoothly through these levels.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Recall the KLM formula.** *(1 mark)*

$$T_{\text{task}} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$$

**Step 2 — Identify and tabulate each operator.** *(1 mark)*

| Operator | Symbol | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|
| Mental recognize | $T_{M,1}$ | 1.20 |
| Home hand | $T_H$ | 0.40 |
| Point to ACK | $T_P$ | 1.10 |
| Click | $T_K$ | 0.28 |
| Mental verify | $T_{M,2}$ | 1.20 |
| Response wait | $T_R$ | 0.50 |
| **Total** | — | **4.68 s** |

**Step 3 — Apply the KLM rule for ordering mental operators.** *(1 mark)*

The two $T_M$ operators are placed *immediately before* the physical actions they prepare, not stacked at the start. Hence the sequence is $M, H, P, K, M, R$, which gives 4.68 s as computed.

**Step 4 — Compare to the deadline.** *(1 mark)*

$$T_{\text{task}} = 4.68\ \text{s} > 2.5\ \text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{DEADLINE MISSED by 2.18 s}$$

**Step 5 — Design improvement 1: Hardware ACK button.** *(1 mark)*

Replace the mouse-based ACK with a **dedicated physical ACK button** on the console. This eliminates the $T_P$ and reduces $T_K$ to ≈ 0.08 s (tactile switch). New task time:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 1.20 + 0.40 + 0.08 + 1.20 + 0.50 = 3.38\ \text{s}$$

Still over 2.5 s.

**Step 6 — Design improvement 2: Reduce mental operators via automatic pre-acknowledgment logic.** *(1 mark)*

Introduce a **"First-Out Auto-ACK"** subsystem that pre-validates the alarm type. This reduces the first $T_M$ from 1.20 s to ≈ 0.30 s (recognition only, no decision). Revised time:

$$T_{\text{final}} = 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.08 + 1.20 + 0.50 = 2.48\ \text{s} < 2.5\ \text{s} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Justify the improvements.** *(1 mark)*

The two improvements follow the KTU-recommended design heuristic: **shift cognitive load from operator to automation** for time-critical tasks, and **replace slow pointing with fast tactile input** for sub-second response requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dead-time term** in the FOPDT formula. Writing $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ is *incomplete* and will cost **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up units** of $K_p$. If $K_p$ has units of $^\circ$C / %, then the final answer must be in $^\circ$C, not "%" or "dimensionless". Always state units.
> 3. **KLM ordering errors.** Students commonly place *all* $T_M$ operators at the start, violating the "M before the action it prepares" rule. The CPM-GOMS variant enforces strict interleaving.
> 4. **Settling time confusion.** $4\tau$ is the **2%** settling time, $3\tau$ is the **5%** settling time. Confusing these costs full sub-part marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the safety/E-stop layer** when drawing the HMI block diagram. The KTU examiner specifically looks for the dashed independent path bypassing the HMI to actuators. Missing it = 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Calling the HMI a "controller".** The HMI is a *supervisory visualization and command entry* layer. The actual controller is the PLC/RTU. This distinction is graded strictly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Process** in HMI is the *real-world operation* being monitored; the HMI is its *symbolic representation*.
- ✅ The **FOPDT model** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$ is the **single most important equation** for this module.
- ✅ The **63.2% rule** at $t = \theta_d + \tau$ and the **2% settling time** $t_s = \theta_d + 4\tau$ are exam favourites.
- ✅ The **ISA-95 hierarchy** has 4 (or 5) levels: Process → Basic Control → SCADA/HMI → MES → ERP.
- ✅ The **KLM equation** $T_{\text{task}} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$ is the standard tool for predicting *operator task time* and identifying *HMI redesign opportunities*.
- ✅ **Dead-time dominant** processes ($\theta_d / \tau > 1$) require **predictive HMI features** to maintain operator trust and situation awareness.
- ✅ The **HMI trend refresh interval** should be ≤ $\tau / 5$ to satisfy visual Nyquist sampling.
- ✅ The **safety/E-stop layer** must be *independent* of the HMI software — a hard-wired physical path from sensor to actuator.
- ✅ HMI supports **Endsley's 3-level SA**: Perception (Level 1–2 HMI), Comprehension (Level 3 trends/alarms), Projection (Level 4 predictive displays).
- ✅ Distinguish carefully between **Process Variable (PV)**, **Setpoint (SP)**, **Controlled Variable (CV)**, and **Manipulated Variable (MV)** — examiners frequently test these definitions.
- ✅ For multivariable processes, use **state-space** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$; for SISO, FOPDT suffices.
- ✅ Always include **units** in numerical answers; **always include a block diagram** in long-answer questions worth ≥ 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scenarios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT724 - ADVANCED HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scenarios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI)**, a **Scenario** is a *narrative, descriptive story-like representation* of how one or more users (actors) perform a specific task or goal-oriented activity by interacting with a proposed or existing system. It is a concrete, situated, and contextualized description of human-computer interaction that captures user actions, system responses, environmental context, and temporal sequencing.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, scenarios are formally classified as a **Requirements Capture & Design Specification Tool** within the *User-Centered Design (UCD)* framework. They serve as a bridge between abstract use cases (which are highly formal) and concrete prototypes (which are highly tangible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A scenario is an informal narrative description that tells the story of how a user (or group of users) accomplishes a meaningful goal through interaction with a system, including the context of use, exceptions, and system feedback."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Movie Storyboard**

Imagine you are a **film director** planning a movie scene. Before shooting even begins, you write a short story describing:
- *Who* the characters are (the user)
- *Where* the action takes place (the context/environment)
- *What* they want to achieve (the goal)
- *How* they go about it (the sequence of actions)
- *What happens next* in response (system feedback)
- *What could go wrong* (exceptions/errors)

A **Scenario in HMI is exactly this — a "storyboard" of interaction** between a human and a machine. Just as a director's storyboard guides the entire film production, a scenario guides the entire UI/UX design process. It is informal, readable, evocative, and shared across designers, developers, and stakeholders — bridging the gap between technical jargon and human storytelling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Scenarios are *design artifacts*, not *technical specifications*. They are deliberately written in natural language to be understood by all stakeholders — engineers, managers, end-users, and clients alike.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

Unlike physics, HMI design relies on **usability metrics** rather than physical constants. The standard quantitative benchmarks tied to scenario evaluation are:

- **Effectiveness:** Number of goals successfully achieved (typically $\geq \mathbf{78\%}$ for acceptable usability per ISO 9241-11).
- **Efficiency:** Time taken per task (measured in **seconds** or **minutes**).
- **Error Rate:** Failures per scenario execution (target $\leq \mathbf{5\%}$).
- **Learnability:** Number of repetitions required before proficiency (target $\leq \mathbf{3}$ trials).

> [!NOTE]
> The **ISO 9241-11 standard** defines the canonical metrics framework used in scenario-based usability testing in KTU board examinations.

---

## 1.4 Why Scenarios Matter in HMI Design

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Communication** | Bridges developers, designers, and non-technical stakeholders. |
| **Early Validation** | Reveals design flaws *before* costly implementation. |
| **Requirement Elicitation** | Uncovers implicit/hidden user needs. |
| **Test Case Generation** | Each scenario maps directly to a usability test case. |
| **Iterative Refinement** | Supports the iterative design-evaluation cycle of UCD. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Scenario (Anatomy)

Every well-formed scenario contains **six essential components**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain these in board examinations.

**Step 1 — Identify the Actor (User Persona)**
- *Why:* Defines *who* interacts with the system.
- *How:* Use personas — fictional yet realistic archetypes (e.g., "Priya, 28, a working professional using a banking app daily").

**Step 2 — Establish the Setting (Context of Use)**
- *Why:* The same user behaves differently in different environments.
- *How:* Specify physical location, device, lighting, noise, social context.

**Step 3 — Define the Goal (Intention)**
- *Why:* A scenario without a goal is mere description.
- *How:* State the user's intended outcome (e.g., "transfer ₹5000 to a friend").

**Step 4 — Sequence the Actions (Plot)**
- *Why:* Captures the *flow* of interaction.
- *How:* Enumerate user actions and corresponding system responses step-by-step.

**Step 5 — Include Exceptions and Errors**
- *Why:* Real-world use is never perfect.
- *How:* Add alternate paths, error states, recovery procedures.

**Step 6 — Capture Outcomes and Success Criteria**
- *Why:* Defines when the scenario is "done."
- *How:* Specify measurable success indicators.

---

## 2.2 Types of Scenarios (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The KTU syllabus classifies scenarios into **four canonical types**, frequently appearing in 14-mark questions.

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Current-State Scenario** | Describes *how things work today* (baseline). | "User currently writes checks manually to pay bills." |
| 2 | **Future-State Scenario** | Describes *how the proposed system will work*. | "User taps 'Pay Bill' and confirms with biometric authentication." |
| 3 | **Evaluation Scenario** | Used during usability testing to assess design. | "Tester must complete a 4-step checkout under 60 seconds." |
| 4 | **Aspirational / Extreme Scenario** | Explores edge cases, future possibilities, or unusual users. | "User pays bills using voice command in a moving car." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "explain the types of scenarios with examples," always use a **single consistent domain** (e.g., banking or healthcare) across all four types for coherence and higher marks.

---

## 2.3 Relationship: Scenarios ↔ Use Cases ↔ User Stories

A frequent KTU exam question asks students to *differentiate* these three artifacts.

| Attribute | Scenario | Use Case | User Story |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Informal narrative | Semi-formal | Highly concise (one-liner) |
| **Length** | Long (paragraph form) | Medium (structured steps) | Short (1–2 sentences) |
| **Audience** | All stakeholders | Developers & analysts | Agile teams |
| **Context** | Rich, situated | Abstract | Goal-oriented |
| **Format** | Prose + actors | UML diagram | "As a... I want... So that..." |

---

## 2.4 Scenario-Based Design (SBD) Methodology

**Scenario-Based Design (SBD)** is a methodology credited to **John M. Carroll** (a foundational figure in HCI referenced in the KTU syllabus). It consists of **four iterative phases**:

1. **Analysis** — Analyze current problems, observe users, identify root causes.
2. **Design** — Generate multiple future-state scenarios, brainstorm alternatives.
3. **Prototyping & Implementation** — Build artifacts that realize the chosen scenario.
4. **Evaluation & Iteration** — Test the scenario with real users, refine.

> [!NOTE]
> The SBD loop is fundamentally **iterative** — each cycle deepens design fidelity. The KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment often tests the *cyclic nature* of this process.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While scenarios are mostly qualitative, the following quantitative relationships appear in board questions:

$$
\text{Task Completion Rate (TCR)} = \frac{\text{Number of Scenarios Successfully Completed}}{\text{Total Number of Scenarios Attempted}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Average Time-on-Task (ATT)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i
$$

$$
\text{Error Rate (ER)} = \frac{\text{Number of Errors}}{\text{Number of Actions Performed}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Scenario Coverage (SC)} = \frac{\vert \text{Implemented Scenarios} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Identified Scenarios} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{System Usability Scale (SUS)} \in [0, 100] \quad \text{(SUS score} \geq \mathbf{68} \text{ indicates acceptable usability)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Compliance:** All set/cardinality notations use `\vert` rather than `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Target Value (Industry Standard) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Task Completion Rate | TCR | % | $\geq 78\%$ |
| Average Time-on-Task | ATT | seconds | Domain-dependent |
| Error Rate | ER | % | $\leq 5\%$ |
| Scenario Coverage | SC | % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| System Usability Scale | SUS | score (0–100) | $\geq 68$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scenario-driven design is employed across industries:

- **Automotive HMI:** Tesla's touchscreen interfaces are designed against thousands of driving scenarios.
- **Aviation Cockpits:** Airbus pilots train on scenario-based simulators covering normal, abnormal, and emergency states.
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical robots are validated against clinical scenarios for FDA approval.
- **Mobile UX:** Apps like Google Pay, Zomato, and IRCTC are built on persona-scenario matrices.
- **Smart Home:** Voice assistants (Alexa, Google Home) handle millions of household scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How to Construct a Scenario from Raw Requirements

The KTU 2024 board exam frequently asks: *"Given a requirement specification, construct a detailed scenario."* Below is the exhaustive 7-step derivation logic.

**Step 1 — Identify the User Persona**

$$
\text{Persona} = \{\text{Name}, \text{Age}, \text{Occupation}, \text{Technical Proficiency}, \text{Goals}, \text{Frustrations}\}
$$

*Logic:* A persona makes the abstract user concrete. Without a persona, scenarios become generic and lose persuasive power.

**Step 2 — Specify the Context of Use**

$$
\text{Context} = \{\text{Device}, \text{Environment}, \text{Network}, \text{Time Pressure}, \text{Social Setting}\}
$$

*Logic:* Context fundamentally alters interaction. A "transfer money" scenario on a smartphone in a crowded train differs radically from one on a desktop in a quiet office.

**Step 3 — Define the Goal**

$$
\text{Goal} \subseteq \text{User Needs} \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Goal} \neq \text{Task}
$$

*Logic:* A *goal* is the user-intent ("send money to friend"). A *task* is the mechanism ("tap contacts, select name, enter amount, confirm"). Scenarios must focus on goals; tasks emerge from goal-pursuit.

**Step 4 — Linearize the Action Sequence**

$$
\text{Scenario} = \{A_1, S_1, A_2, S_2, A_3, S_3, \ldots, A_n, S_n\}
$$

where $A_i$ = user action at step $i$ and $S_i$ = system response at step $i$.

*Logic:* Scenarios unfold as alternating turns. This bi-directional turn-taking is the fundamental grammar of HCI scenarios.

**Step 5 — Embed Exception Paths**

$$
\text{Scenario}_{\text{full}} = \text{Scenario}_{\text{happy}} \cup \{\text{Exception}_1, \text{Exception}_2, \ldots\}
$$

*Logic:* Real systems fail. A scenario without exceptions is a fairy tale, not an engineering artifact. KTU examiners award full marks only when exceptions are explicitly addressed.

**Step 6 — Define Success Criteria**

$$
\text{Success} \iff (\text{Goal Achieved}) \land (\text{TCR} \geq 78\%) \land (\text{ER} \leq 5\%) \land (\text{ATT} \leq \text{Threshold})
$$

*Logic:* Success must be *measurable*. Vague success criteria invalidate the scenario for empirical usability testing.

**Step 7 — Cross-Validate Against Personas and Use Cases**

$$
\text{Validation: } \vert \text{Scenarios} \vert \geq k \cdot \vert \text{Personas} \vert, \quad k \geq 2
$$

*Logic:* A minimum of 2 scenarios per persona is required to cover at least one happy-path and one exception-path. This is the KTU-recommended *coverage rule*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Scenario Coverage Analysis

A common KTU 14-mark sub-question asks to build a **Persona × Scenario Matrix** to evaluate design completeness.

| Persona \\ Scenario | S1: New User Onboarding | S2: Routine Task | S3: Error Recovery | S4: Power Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1: Novice User** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| **P2: Regular User** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **P3: Power User** | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **P4: Elderly User** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |

*Reading the matrix:* Cell marked ✓ means the persona-scenario combination is explicitly designed. Empty cells (—) indicate *coverage gaps* — which designers must address in subsequent iterations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage Rule:** A scenario matrix is considered *complete* if and only if every cell is filled, i.e., $\forall P_i, S_j: \text{Cell}(P_i, S_j) \neq \text{—}$. This is a high-yield KTU evaluation criterion.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Scenario-Based Usability Metrics

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A usability test was conducted on a new mobile banking app with 5 users, each performing 4 scenarios. The following raw data was collected:

- Total scenarios attempted: $5 \times 4 = 20$
- Scenarios completed successfully: 17
- Total time taken (sum): $3{,}240$ seconds
- Total errors observed: 8
- Total actions performed (across all users): 320

**Compute:** (a) Task Completion Rate, (b) Average Time-on-Task, (c) Error Rate, (d) Comment on usability per ISO 9241-11.

**Exhaustive Solution:**

### Part (a) — Task Completion Rate

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Successful Completions}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{17}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{TCR} = 0.85 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCR} = 85\%}
$$

Since $85\% \geq 78\%$, the **effectiveness criterion is satisfied**.

### Part (b) — Average Time-on-Task

$$
\text{ATT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = \frac{3{,}240 \text{ s}}{20}
$$

$$
\text{ATT} = 162 \text{ seconds per scenario}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ATT} = 162 \text{ s}}
$$

### Part (c) — Error Rate

$$
\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Total Errors}}{\text{Total Actions}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ER} = \frac{8}{320} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ER} = 0.025 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ER} = 2.5\%}
$$

Since $2.5\% \leq 5\%$, the **efficiency criterion is satisfied**.

### Part (d) — Usability Verdict

Effectiveness = 85% (pass), Error Rate = 2.5% (pass), hence the system is **usable** under ISO 9241-11 with a remark that the *time efficiency* should be benchmarked against competitor apps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Scenario-Based Design Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[user1 Research and Observation] --> B[step1 Problem Analysis]
    B --> C[step2 Current State Scenarios]
    C --> D[step3 Design Ideation]
    D --> E[step4 Future State Scenarios]
    E --> F[step5 Prototype Construction]
    F --> G[step6 Usability Evaluation]
    G --> H{step7 Goals Achieved}
    H -- Yes --> I[step8 Deployment and Refinement]
    H -- No --> D
    I --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The lifecycle begins with user research, flows through scenario generation in two states (current vs. future), proceeds to prototyping and evaluation, and conditionally loops back to ideation if usability goals are unmet. The KTU board frequently asks students to label the **feedback loop** — note the path $G \rightarrow D$ representing iterative refinement.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of a Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[actor1 Actor Persona] --> B[ctx1 Context of Use]
    B --> C[goal1 Goal Statement]
    C --> D[act1 Action Sequence]
    D --> E[sys1 System Response]
    E --> F[exc1 Exception Paths]
    F --> G[succ1 Success Criteria]
    G --> H[out1 Outcome]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This block-level anatomy shows the linear flow of scenario construction. Each block must be filled for the scenario to be considered *complete* by KTU 2024 standards.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Persona × Scenario Coverage Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph p1[Persona Layer]
        P1[psn1 Novice]
        P2[psn2 Regular]
        P3[psn3 Power]
    end
    subgraph s1[Scenario Layer]
        S1[scn1 Onboarding]
        S2[scn2 Routine Task]
        S3[scn3 Error Recovery]
        S4[scn4 Advanced Use]
    end
    P1 --- S1
    P1 --- S2
    P1 --- S3
    P2 --- S1
    P2 --- S2
    P2 --- S3
    P2 --- S4
    P3 --- S2
    P3 --- S3
    P3 --- S4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Edges between Persona and Scenario nodes represent designed interaction flows. Missing edges (e.g., Novice → Advanced Use) indicate intentional design gaps that the design team has documented as out-of-scope for the current iteration.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Scenario Exception Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Happy Path: Goal Achievement]) --> Step1[act1 User Initiates Action]
    Step1 --> Step2[act2 System Processes]
    Step2 --> Step3{cond1 Validation}
    Step3 -- Success --> Step4[act4 Goal Completed]
    Step3 -- Failure --> Err1[err1 Error Dialog]
    Err1 --> Rec1[rec1 Retry Option]
    Err1 --> Rec2[rec2 Cancel and Exit]
    Step4 --> End([Outcome Achieved])
    Rec1 --> Step1
    Rec2 --> End
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision-tree shows the dichotomy between the *happy path* (success flow) and *exception paths* (error-handling flows). The KTU examiner awards bonus marks if students include both the retry and cancel branches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a "Scenario" in the context of Human-Machine Interface design. Mention any two components of a scenario.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **scenario** in HMI design is an *informal, narrative description* of how a specific user (or group of users) accomplishes a meaningful goal by interacting with a system within a defined context. It captures user actions, system responses, and the environment in which interaction takes place. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> Two key components are: **(1.5 marks)**
> 1. **Actor (User Persona)** — a representative description of the user performing the task.
> 2. **Goal** — the desired outcome the user aims to achieve.
>
> *(Other acceptable components: Context, Action Sequence, System Response, Exception Paths, Success Criteria.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between a Current-State Scenario and a Future-State Scenario with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Current-State Scenario | Future-State Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Describes existing interaction problems. | Describes the proposed system's interaction. |
| **Tone** | Diagnostic (identifies pain points). | Aspirational (defines ideal flow). |
| **Usage** | Baseline for redesign. | Blueprint for development. |

**Examples (1 mark each):**
- *Current-State:* "The user manually writes cheques and visits the bank to pay electricity bills, often waiting 30 minutes in queue." **(1 mark)**
- *Future-State:* "The user opens a mobile banking app, taps 'Pay Bill', selects 'Electricity', confirms with biometric, and receives an instant SMS receipt." **(1 mark)**

*(Award full 3 marks only if both table and examples are present.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four major types of scenarios used in HMI design with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Scenario-Based Design (SBD) methodology** proposed by John M. Carroll. List its four phases and explain the role of iteration.

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Four Types of Scenarios (7 marks)

The four canonical types are: **(7 marks — 1.5 marks per type, 1 mark for example)**

1. **Current-State Scenarios (1.5 marks):**
   - These describe the *as-is* situation — how users currently accomplish a task using existing tools, often inefficiently.
   - *Purpose:* Identify pain points, bottlenecks, and unmet needs.
   - *Example:* "Ramesh, a college student, currently checks his attendance by walking to the notice board and manually tallying his absences against the printed register."

2. **Future-State Scenarios (1.5 marks):**
   - These describe the *to-be* situation — how the proposed system will enable the user to accomplish the same task efficiently.
   - *Purpose:* Communicate the design vision to stakeholders and developers.
   - *Example:* "Ramesh opens the college mobile app, logs in with his roll number, and instantly views a graphical attendance dashboard showing subject-wise percentages."

3. **Evaluation Scenarios (1.5 marks):**
   - These are test scripts used during usability testing to evaluate whether the system meets design goals.
   - *Purpose:* Provide measurable, repeatable tasks for usability studies.
   - *Example:* "Give the participant a printed bill, ask them to pay it via the app, and record the time, errors, and success status."

4. **Aspirational / Extreme Scenarios (1.5 marks):**
   - These explore *edge cases* — unusual users, future technologies, or boundary conditions.
   - *Purpose:* Stretch the design beyond conventional use; identify future-proofing requirements.
   - *Example:* "Visually impaired Ramesh pays his bill using only voice commands and screen-reader feedback while commuting on a noisy bus."

**Synthesis (0.5 mark):** Together, these four scenario types form a complete design narrative — from problem identification to solution envisioning to validation to future expansion.

---

#### Part (b) — Scenario-Based Design Methodology (7 marks)

**Scenario-Based Design (SBD)** is a *user-centered, narrative-driven* HMI design methodology credited to **John M. Carroll** (1995, 2000). It posits that *design is fundamentally a storytelling activity* — designers construct concrete, situated scenarios that anchor abstract requirements in real-world use. **(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for citing Carroll)**

**The Four Phases of SBD (4 marks — 1 per phase):**

1. **Analysis Phase:**
   - Designers observe users in real contexts, conduct task analyses, and document current-state scenarios.
   - Output: Problem statement, persona repository, current-state scenario catalog.

2. **Design Phase:**
   - Designers brainstorm and author multiple future-state scenarios, exploring alternative interaction paradigms.
   - Output: Future-state scenario catalog, design alternatives, sketches, low-fidelity prototypes.

3. **Prototyping & Implementation Phase:**
   - Selected scenarios are realized as high-fidelity prototypes or production-ready systems.
   - Output: Interactive prototype, system implementation, test-ready artifact.

4. **Evaluation & Iteration Phase:**
   - Evaluation scenarios are executed with real users; metrics (TCR, ATT, ER) are measured.
   - Output: Usability test report, design recommendations, refined scenarios.

**Role of Iteration (1 mark):**
The four phases are *not linear* but form a **cyclic loop**. The insights from evaluation feed back into the design phase, generating new scenarios or refining existing ones. This iterative refinement ensures that the final system remains anchored in real user needs rather than designer assumptions.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
SBD is particularly powerful because it keeps the design process *human-readable* throughout. Unlike formal specifications, scenarios remain accessible to non-technical stakeholders, ensuring that the final system reflects a shared understanding of the user's journey.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct a detailed future-state scenario for a **smart healthcare appointment booking system** for elderly patients. Include all six essential components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A usability test was conducted on the above system with 8 users performing 5 scenarios each. The results were: 32 scenarios completed successfully, total time 4800 seconds, total errors 25, total actions 800. Calculate the Task Completion Rate, Average Time-on-Task, and Error Rate. Comment on the system's usability.

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Detailed Future-State Scenario (7 marks)

**Title:** "Mrs. Lakshmi Books a Cardiology Appointment Using the Smart Healthcare App"

**Component 1 — Actor (1 mark):**
Mrs. Lakshmi, a 67-year-old retired school teacher, lives alone in Kochi. She has mild arthritis in her hands and limited experience with smartphones. She wears reading glasses and speaks Malayalam as her primary language.

**Component 2 — Context of Use (1 mark):**
It is a Sunday morning at her home. She is sitting on her sofa, holding her smartphone (5.5-inch screen, Android 13). The room is quiet, well-lit, and she has a cup of tea nearby. The phone is connected to home Wi-Fi.

**Component 3 — Goal (1 mark):**
Mrs. Lakshmi wants to book a cardiology consultation with Dr. Menon at Sunrise Hospital for next Wednesday, preferably in the morning slot.

**Component 4 — Action Sequence (1.5 marks):**
1. She unlocks the phone with fingerprint.
2. She taps the "HealthApp" icon (large, high-contrast).
3. The home screen displays a voice prompt in Malayalam: "How can I help you?"
4. She taps the "Book Appointment" button.
5. She selects "Cardiology" from a list of specialties.
6. She chooses "Dr. Menon" from a list of available doctors with photos.
7. The calendar shows next Wednesday highlighted; she taps it.
8. Available time slots (9:00 AM, 10:30 AM, 2:00 PM) appear; she selects 9:00 AM.
9. The app displays a confirmation summary in large Malayalam text.
10. She taps "Confirm" and the appointment is booked.

**Component 5 — Exception Paths (1 mark):**
- If the Wi-Fi fails at step 4, the app displays "No Internet" in Malayalam and offers a "Retry" button.
- If the 9:00 AM slot is already taken, the app shows "Slot Unavailable" and suggests the next available slot.
- If Mrs. Lakshmi taps the wrong specialty, a "Back" button is prominently available.

**Component 6 — Success Criteria & Outcome (1.5 marks):**
- The appointment is confirmed, and an SMS is sent to her registered mobile.
- An entry appears in her "My Appointments" list with date, time, doctor name, and hospital address.
- The interaction took less than 3 minutes with zero errors.
- Mrs. Lakshmi expresses satisfaction: "It was very easy, I could do it myself."

---

#### Part (b) — Usability Metric Computation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Users: 8
- Scenarios per user: 5
- Total attempts: $8 \times 5 = 40$
- Successful completions: 32
- Total time: 4800 seconds
- Total errors: 25
- Total actions: 800

**Part (b.1) — Task Completion Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{32}{40} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{TCR} = 0.80 \times 100\% = \boxed{80\%}
$$

Since $80\% \geq 78\%$, the **effectiveness criterion is satisfied**.

**Part (b.2) — Average Time-on-Task (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ATT} = \frac{4800 \text{ s}}{40} = \boxed{120 \text{ seconds per scenario}}
$$

**Part (b.3) — Error Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ER} = \frac{25}{800} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ER} = 0.03125 \times 100\% = \boxed{3.125\%}
$$

Since $3.125\% \leq 5\%$, the **error rate criterion is satisfied**.

**Part (b.4) — Usability Verdict (1 mark):**

The system demonstrates **acceptable usability** per ISO 9241-11 standards. Both effectiveness (80%) and efficiency (error rate 3.125%) meet the established thresholds. However, the design team should investigate the 8 failed scenarios to identify whether they cluster around specific user groups (e.g., elderly users with severe arthritis) or specific task steps, and refine the interface accordingly in the next iteration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting exception paths:** Many students describe only the *happy path*. Always include at least 2 exception branches for full marks.
> 2. **Confusing Goal with Task:** A goal is the *user's intention*; a task is the *mechanism*. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 3. **Skipping iteration in SBD:** A common 2-mark deduction occurs when students describe SBD as a *linear* process. SBD is fundamentally **cyclic** — emphasize iteration explicitly.
> 4. **Unit mistakes in ATT:** Always state the unit (seconds/minutes). Writing "ATT = 120" without a unit costs 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Not citing ISO 9241-11:** When commenting on usability, always reference the standard for academic rigor.
> 6. **Vague personas:** "A user" is not a persona. Use a *named* persona with demographic and psychographic details.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scenarios Module:**

- **Definition:** A scenario is a *narrative description* of user–system interaction in a specific context.
- **Six Components:** Actor + Context + Goal + Action Sequence + Exceptions + Success Criteria.
- **Four Types:** Current-State, Future-State, Evaluation, Aspirational/Extreme.
- **SBD Phases:** Analysis → Design → Prototyping → Evaluation (cyclic, not linear).
- **SBD Originator:** John M. Carroll (must cite in 14-mark answers).
- **Persona vs. Scenario:** A persona is *who*; a scenario is *what they do*.
- **Goal vs. Task:** Goal = *intent*; Task = *mechanism*.
